**Hebron**[[a]](#cite_note-7) is a city in the southern [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), Palestine, and the capital of the [Hebron Governorate](/wiki/Hebron_Governorate "Hebron Governorate"), which is the largest in the West Bank. It is located 30 kilometers (19 mi) south of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"). The population inside in the city limits is 201,063, while the adjacent metropolitan area within the governorate is home to over 700,000 people. Hebron spans across an area of 74.1 square kilometers (28.6 sq mi). It is the [third largest city in the country](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Palestine "List of cities in Palestine") after [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") and [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"). The city is often considered one of the [Four Holy Cities](/wiki/Four_Holy_Cities "Four Holy Cities") in [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), as well as in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").

It is one of the [oldest continuously inhabited cities](/wiki/List_of_oldest_continuously_inhabited_cities "List of oldest continuously inhabited cities") in the Levant. According to the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") settled in Hebron and bought the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs") as a burial place for his wife [Sarah](/wiki/Sarah "Sarah"). Biblical tradition holds that the patriarchs Abraham, [Isaac](/wiki/Isaac "Isaac"), and [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob"), along with their wives Sarah, [Rebecca](/wiki/Rebecca_(biblical_figure) "Rebecca (biblical figure)"), and [Leah](/wiki/Leah "Leah"), were buried in the cave. The city is also recognized in the Bible as the place where [David](/wiki/David "David") was anointed [king of Israel](/wiki/King_of_Israel "King of Israel").

Following the [Babylonian captivity](/wiki/Babylonian_captivity "Babylonian captivity"), the [Edomites](/wiki/Edom "Edom") settled in Hebron. During the first century BCE, [Herod the Great](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great") built the wall that still surrounds the Cave of the Patriarchs, which later became a [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)"), and then [a mosque](/wiki/Ibrahimi_Mosque "Ibrahimi Mosque"). With the exception of a [brief Crusader control](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"), successive Muslim dynasties ruled Hebron from the 7th century CE until the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")'s [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Dissolution of the Ottoman Empire") following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), when the city became part of [British Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine").

The [1929 Hebron massacre](/wiki/1929_Hebron_massacre "1929 Hebron massacre") of nearly 70 Jews and the [Arab uprising of 1936–39](/wiki/1936%E2%80%931939_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine") led the British government to evacuate the Jewish community from Hebron. The [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War") saw the entire West Bank, including Hebron, [occupied and annexed](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") by [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), and since the 1967 [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), the city has been under [Israeli control](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank"). Following Israeli occupation, Jewish presence was restored in the city. Since the 1997 [Hebron Protocol](/wiki/Protocol_Concerning_the_Redeployment_in_Hebron "Protocol Concerning the Redeployment in Hebron"), most of Hebron has been governed by the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority"). The city is often described as a "microcosm" of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict") and the [Israeli occupation of the West Bank](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank"). The 1997 protocol divided the city into two sectors—H1 Hebron, governed by the Palestinian National Authority, and H2 Hebron, occupied by Israeli authorities. All security arrangements and travel permits for local residents are coordinated between the Palestinian Authority and Israel via the [COGAT](/wiki/Coordinator_of_Government_Activities_in_the_Territories "Coordinator of Government Activities in the Territories"). The Jewish settlers have their own governing municipal body, the Committee of the Jewish Community of Hebron.

The largest city in the southern West Bank, Hebron is a chief commercial and industrial center in the region. It is a busy hub of trade, generating roughly a third of the area's [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"), largely due to the sale of [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") from quarries in its area. Hebron has a local reputation for its grapes, figs, ceramics, plastics, [pottery](/wiki/Palestinian_pottery "Palestinian pottery") workshops, metalworking and [glassblowing](/wiki/Hebron_glass "Hebron glass") industry. The city is home to numerous shopping malls. The [Old City of Hebron](/wiki/Old_City_of_Hebron "Old City of Hebron") features narrow, winding streets, flat-roofed stone houses, and old [bazaars](/wiki/Bazaar "Bazaar"). It is recognized as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). Hebron is also known as a regional educational and medical hub.

## Etymology

The name *Hebron* appears to trace back to two [northwest Semitic languages](/wiki/Northwest_Semitic_languages "Northwest Semitic languages"), which coalesce in the form *[ḥbr](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D7%97%D6%BE%D7%91%D6%BE%D7%A8 "wikt:ח־ב־ר")*,[[b]](#cite_note-9) having reflexes in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") and [Amorite](/wiki/Amorite "Amorite"), with a basic sense of 'unite' and connoting a range of meanings from 'colleague' to 'friend'. In the proper name *Hebron*, the original sense may have been 'alliance'.[[8]](#cite_note-10)

Early Muslim sources refer to the city as *Ḥabra* (حبرة) or *Ḥabrūn* (حبرون).[[9]](#cite_note-GR-11) *al-Khalīl* emerged as the Arabic name for the city in the 13th century,[[9]](#cite_note-GR-11) derived from the [Qur'anic](/wiki/Quran "Quran") epithet for [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham_in_Islam "Abraham in Islam"), *Khalīl al-Raḥmān* ('Friend of [the Merciful](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Islam "Names of God in Islam") [i.e. [God](/wiki/God_in_Islam "God in Islam")]').[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13) Thus, the epithet is a [calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque") of the original Hebrew [toponym](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym") *Ḥeḇrōn*, understood as *ḥaḇēr* ('friend').[[12]](#cite_note-Sharon_2007_104-14)

## History

See also: [Timeline of Hebron](/wiki/Timeline_of_Hebron "Timeline of Hebron")

### Bronze and Iron Ages

Archaeological excavations reveal traces of strong fortifications dated to the Early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), covering some 24–30 [dunams](/wiki/Dunam "Dunam") centered around [Tel Rumeida](/wiki/Tel_Rumeida "Tel Rumeida"). The city flourished in the 17th–18th centuries BCE before being destroyed by fire, and was resettled in the late Middle Bronze Age.[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) This older Hebron was originally a [Canaanite](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") royal city.[[15]](#cite_note-17) [Abrahamic legend](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") associates the city with the [Hittites](/wiki/Biblical_Hittites "Biblical Hittites").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] It has been conjectured that Hebron might have been the capital of [Shuwardata](/wiki/%C5%A0uwardata "Šuwardata") of [Gath](/wiki/Gath_(city) "Gath (city)"), an [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans") contemporary of Jerusalem's regent, [Abdi-Ḫeba](/wiki/Abdi-Heba "Abdi-Heba"),[[16]](#cite_note-18) although the Hebron hills were almost devoid of settlements in the Late Bronze Age.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

### Ancient Israel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Hebron136.JPG/250px-Hebron136.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hebron136.JPG)

Excavations at [Tel Rumeida](/wiki/Tel_Rumeida "Tel Rumeida")

The [Abrahamic traditions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religion "Abrahamic religion") associated with Hebron are nomadic. This may also reflect a [Kenite](/wiki/Kenite "Kenite") element, since the nomadic Kenites are said to have long occupied the city,[[18]](#cite_note-20) and *Heber* is the name for a Kenite clan.[[19]](#cite_note-21) In the narrative of the later Hebrew conquest, Hebron was one of two centers under Canaanite control. They were ruled by the three sons of [Anak](/wiki/Anak "Anak") (*benê/yelîdê hāʿănaq*).[[20]](#cite_note-22) or may reflect some Kenite and [Kenizzite](/wiki/Kenizzite "Kenizzite") migration from the Negev to Hebron, since terms related to the Kenizzites appear to be close to [Hurrian](/wiki/Hurrian_language "Hurrian language"). This suggests that behind the [Anakim](/wiki/Anakim "Anakim") legend lies some early Hurrian population.[[21]](#cite_note-23) In Biblical lore they are represented as descendants of the [Nephilim](/wiki/Nephilim "Nephilim").[[22]](#cite_note-24) The [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") mentions that it was formerly called [Kiryath-arba](/wiki/Kiryat_Arba "Kiryat Arba"), or "city of four", possibly referring to the four pairs or couples who were buried there, or four tribes, or four quarters,[[23]](#cite_note-25) four hills,[[24]](#cite_note-26) or a confederated settlement of four families.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

The story of Abraham's purchase of the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs") from the [Hittites](/wiki/Biblical_Hittites "Biblical Hittites") constitutes a seminal element in what was to become the Jewish attachment to the land[[26]](#cite_note-28) in that it signified the first "real estate" of Israel long before the conquest under Joshua.[[27]](#cite_note-29) In settling here, Abraham is described as making his first [covenant](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/covenant "wiktionary:covenant"), an alliance with two local [Amorite](/wiki/Amorite "Amorite") clans who became his *ba'alei brit* or *masters of the covenant*.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

The Hebron of the Israelites was centered on what is now known as Tel Rumeida, while its ritual center was located at [Elonei Mamre](/wiki/Mamre "Mamre").[[29]](#cite_note-31) Hebrew Bible narrative also describes the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Samson_Fenster_aus_Alpirsbach.jpg/250px-Samson_Fenster_aus_Alpirsbach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samson_Fenster_aus_Alpirsbach.jpg)

[Samson](/wiki/Samson "Samson") removes gates of Gaza (left) and brings them to Mount Hebron (right). Strassburg (1160–1170), [Württemberg State Museum](/wiki/Landesmuseum_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Landesmuseum Württemberg") in Stuttgart

It is said to have been wrested from the Canaanites by either [Joshua](/wiki/Joshua "Joshua"), who is said to have wiped out all of its previous inhabitants, "destroying everything that drew breath, as the Lord God of Israel had commanded",[[30]](#cite_note-32) or the [Tribe of Judah](/wiki/Tribe_of_Judah "Tribe of Judah") as a whole, or specifically [Caleb](/wiki/Caleb "Caleb") the Judahite.[[31]](#cite_note-33) The town itself, with some contiguous pasture land, is then said to have been granted to the [Levites](/wiki/Levites "Levites") of the clan of [Kohath](/wiki/Kohathites "Kohathites"), while the fields of the city, as well as its surrounding villages were assigned to Caleb ([Joshua 21:3–12; 1 Chronicles 6:54–56](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Joshua+21:3–12;+1+Chronicles+6:54–56&version=NRSV)),[[32]](#cite_note-34) who expels the three giants, [Sheshai](/wiki/Sheshai "Sheshai"), [Ahiman](/wiki/Ahiman "Ahiman"), and [Talmai](/wiki/Talmai "Talmai"), who ruled the city. Later, the biblical narrative has [King David](/wiki/King_David "King David") called by God to relocate to Hebron and reign from there for some seven years ([2 Samuel 2:1–3](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=2%20Samuel%202:1–3&version=nrsv)).[[33]](#cite_note-FritzDavies19962-35) It is there that the elders of Israel come to him to make a covenant before Elohim and anoint him [king of Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(united_monarchy) "Kingdom of Israel (united monarchy)").[[34]](#cite_note-36) It was in Hebron again that [Absalom](/wiki/Absalom "Absalom") has himself declared king and then raises a revolt against his father David ([2 Samuel 15:7–10](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=2%20Samuel%2015:7–10&version=nrsv)). It became one of the principal centers of the Tribe of Judah and was classified as one of the six traditional [Cities of Refuge](/wiki/Cities_of_Refuge "Cities of Refuge").[[35]](#cite_note-37)

As is shown by the discovery at [Lachish](/wiki/Lachish "Lachish"), the second most important city in the [Kingdom of Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah") after Jerusalem,[[36]](#cite_note-38) of seals with the inscription [*lmlk Hebron*](/wiki/LMLK_seal "LMLK seal") (to the king Hebron),[[12]](#cite_note-Sharon_2007_104-14) Hebron continued to constitute an important local economic center, given its strategic position on the crossroads between the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") to the east, Jerusalem to the north, the Negev and Egypt to the south, and the [Shepelah](/wiki/Shfela "Shfela") and the [coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain") to the west.[[37]](#cite_note-39) Lying along [trading routes](/wiki/Way_of_the_Patriarchs "Way of the Patriarchs"), it remained administratively and politically dependent on Jerusalem for this period.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

After the destruction of the [First Temple](/wiki/First_Temple "First Temple"), most of the Jewish inhabitants of Hebron were exiled, and according to the conventional view,[[39]](#cite_note-41) some researchers found traces of [Edomite](/wiki/Edom "Edom") presence after the 5th–4th centuries BCE, as the area became [Achaemenid province](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"),[[40]](#cite_note-42) and, in the wake of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great")'s conquest, Hebron was throughout the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") under the influence of Idumea (as the new area inhabited by the Edomites was called during the [Persian](/wiki/Yehud_medinata "Yehud medinata"), [Hellenistic](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah#Hellenistic_period "History of ancient Israel and Judah") and [Roman](/wiki/Judea_(Roman_province)#Judea_as_Roman_province(s) "Judea (Roman province)") periods), as is attested by inscriptions for that period bearing names with the Edomite God [Qōs](/wiki/Qaus "Qaus").[[41]](#cite_note-43) Jews also appear to have lived there after the return from the [Babylonian exile](/wiki/Babylonian_exile "Babylonian exile") ([Nehemiah 11:25](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Nehemiah%2011:25&version=nrsv)). During the [Maccabean revolt](/wiki/Maccabean_revolt "Maccabean revolt"), Hebron was burnt and plundered by [Judah Maccabee](/wiki/Judas_Maccabeus "Judas Maccabeus") who fought against the Edomites in 167 BCE.[[42]](#cite_note-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45) The city appears to have long resisted [Hasmonean dominance](/wiki/Hasmoneans "Hasmoneans"), however, and indeed as late as the [First Jewish–Roman War](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War") was still considered [Idumean](/wiki/Idumean "Idumean").[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Roman rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs%2C_Hebron_2007.jpg/330px-Cave_of_the_Patriarchs%2C_Hebron_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cave_of_the_Patriarchs,_Hebron_2007.jpg)

[Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs")

The present day city of Hebron was settled in the valley downhill from Tel Rumeida at the latest by Roman times.[[45]](#cite_note-47) [Herod the Great](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great"), king of Judea, built the wall that still surrounds the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs"). During the [First Jewish–Roman War](/wiki/First_Jewish%E2%80%93Roman_War "First Jewish–Roman War"), Hebron was captured and plundered by [Simon Bar Giora](/wiki/Simon_Bar_Giora "Simon Bar Giora"), a leader of the [Zealots](/wiki/Zealots "Zealots"), without bloodshed. The "little town" was later laid to waste by [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian")'s officer [Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis](/wiki/Sextus_Vettulenus_Cerialis "Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis").[[46]](#cite_note-48) [Josephus](/wiki/Josephus "Josephus") wrote that he "slew all he found there, young and old, and burnt down the town". After the suppression of the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") in 135 CE, innumerable Jewish captives were sold into slavery at Hebron's [Terebinth](/wiki/Mamre "Mamre") slave-market.[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50)

The city was part of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") in [Palaestina Prima](/wiki/Palaestina_Prima "Palaestina Prima") province at the [Diocese of the East](/wiki/Diocese_of_the_East "Diocese of the East"). The Byzantine emperor [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") erected a Christian church over the Cave of Machpelah in the 6th century CE, which was later destroyed by the [Sassanid](/wiki/Sassanid "Sassanid") general [Shahrbaraz](/wiki/Shahrbaraz "Shahrbaraz") in 614 when [Khosrau II](/wiki/Khosrau_II "Khosrau II")'s armies besieged and took Jerusalem.[[49]](#cite_note-51) Jews were not permitted to reside in Hebron under Byzantine rule.[[50]](#cite_note-Scharfstein_124-52) The sanctuary itself however was spared by the Persians, in deference to the Jewish population, who were numerous in the [Sassanid army](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sassanid_War_of_602%E2%80%93628 "Byzantine–Sassanid War of 602–628").[[51]](#cite_note-Salaville_1910_185-53)

### Muslim conquest and Islamic caliphate

Hebron was one of the last cities of Palestine to fall to the [Islamic invasion in the 7th century](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant"), possibly the reason why Hebron is not mentioned in any traditions of the Arab conquest.[[52]](#cite_note-54) When the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") established its rule over Hebron in 638, the Muslims converted the Byzantine church at the site of Abraham's tomb into a mosque.[[50]](#cite_note-Scharfstein_124-52) It became an important station on the caravan trading route from Egypt, and also as a way-station for pilgrims making the yearly hajj from Damascus.[[53]](#cite_note-55) After the fall of the city, Jerusalem's conqueror, Caliph [Omar ibn al-Khattab](/wiki/Umar "Umar") permitted Jewish people to return and to construct a small synagogue within the Herodian precinct.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Catholic bishop [Arculf](/wiki/Arculf "Arculf"), who visited the Holy Land during the [Umayyad period](/wiki/Umayyad_Chaliphate "Umayyad Chaliphate"), described the city as unfortified and poor. In his writings he also mentioned camel caravans transporting firewood from Hebron to Jerusalem, which implies there was a presence of Arab nomads in the region at that time.[[55]](#cite_note-57) Trade greatly expanded, in particular with [Bedouins](/wiki/Bedouin "Bedouin") in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") (*al-Naqab*) and the population to the east of the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") (*Baḥr Lūṭ*). According to Anton Kisa, Jews from Hebron (and [Tyre](/wiki/Tyre,_Lebanon "Tyre, Lebanon")) founded the [Venetian glass](/wiki/Venetian_glass "Venetian glass") industry in the 9th century.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

Hebron was almost absent from Muslim literature before the 10th century.[[57]](#cite_note-59) In 985, [al-Muqaddasi](/wiki/Al-Muqaddasi "Al-Muqaddasi") described Hebron (Habra) as the village of Abraham al-Khalil, with a strong fortress and a stone dome over Abraham's sepulchre.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) The mosque contained the tombs of Isaac, Jacob, and their wives.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) Surrounding the area were villages with vineyards producing exceptional grapes and apples.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) Hebron had a public guest house offering lentils and olive oil to both the poor and the rich.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) The guest house was established through the bequest of Prophet Muhammad's companions, including Tamim-al Dari, and received generous donations.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) It was highly regarded as an excellent house of hospitality and charity in the realm of al-Islam.[[58]](#cite_note-:6-60) The custom, known as the 'Table of Abraham' (*simāt al-khalil*), was similar to the one established by the [Fatimids](/wiki/Fatimid "Fatimid").[[59]](#cite_note-:7-61) In 1047, [Nasir-i-Khusraw](/wiki/Nasir_Khusraw "Nasir Khusraw") described Hebron in his [Safarnama](/wiki/Safarnama "Safarnama") as having many villages providing revenues for pious purposes.[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62)[[59]](#cite_note-:7-61) He mentioned a spring flowing from under a stone, with water channeled to a covered tank outside the town.[[59]](#cite_note-:7-61) The Sanctuary stood on the town's southern border, enclosed by four walls.[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62) Barley was the primary crop, with abundant olives.[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62) Visitors were provided with bread, olives, lentils cooked in olive oil, and raisins.[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62) Hebron had numerous mills operated by oxen and mules, along with working girls baking bread.[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62) The hospitality extended to about three-pound loaves of bread and meals for every arriving person, including up to 500 pilgrims on certain days.[[59]](#cite_note-:7-61)[[60]](#cite_note-:8-62)

The tradition survives to this day in the form of the [Takiat Ibrahim](/wiki/Al-Takiya_Al-Ibrahimiya "Al-Takiya Al-Ibrahimiya") soup kitchen, which has been active in providing food for thousands over Ramadan, which coincided with food shortages during the 2024 [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZbeedat2024-63) [Geniza](/wiki/Cairo_Geniza "Cairo Geniza") documents from this period mention "the graves of the patriarchs" and attest to the presence of an organized Jewish community in Hebron. The Jews maintained a synagogue near the tomb and earned their livelihood accommodating Jewish pilgrims and merchants. During the [Seljuk period](/wiki/Great_Seljuq_Empire "Great Seljuq Empire"), the community was headed by Saadia b. Abraham b. Nathan, known as the "*haver* of the graves of the patriarchs."[[62]](#cite_note-64)

### Crusader and Ayyubid period

See also: [Vassals of the Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Vassals_of_the_Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Vassals of the Kingdom of Jerusalem")

The [Caliphate](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") lasted in the area until 1099, when the Christian [Crusader](/wiki/Crusade "Crusade") [Godfrey de Bouillon](/wiki/Godfrey_de_Bouillon "Godfrey de Bouillon") took Hebron and renamed it "Castellion Saint Abraham".[[63]](#cite_note-65) It was designated capital of the southern district of the Crusader [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem")[[64]](#cite_note-66) and given, in turn,[[65]](#cite_note-67) as the fief of Saint Abraham, to [Geldemar Carpinel](/wiki/Geldemar_Carpenel "Geldemar Carpenel"), the bishop Gerard of Avesnes,[[66]](#cite_note-68) Hugh of Rebecques, Walter Mohamet and Baldwin of Saint Abraham. As a [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") garrison of the [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"), its defense was precarious being "little more than an island in a Moslem ocean".[[67]](#cite_note-69) The Crusaders converted the [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") and the [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") into a church. In 1106, an Egyptian campaign thrust into southern Palestine and almost succeeded the following year in wresting Hebron back from the Crusaders under [Baldwin I of Jerusalem](/wiki/Baldwin_I_of_Jerusalem "Baldwin I of Jerusalem"), who personally led the counter-charge to beat the Muslim forces off. In the year 1113 during the reign of [Baldwin II of Jerusalem](/wiki/Baldwin_II_of_Jerusalem "Baldwin II of Jerusalem"), according to [Ali of Herat](/wiki/Ali_ibn_abi_bakr_al-Harawi "Ali ibn abi bakr al-Harawi") (writing in 1173), a certain part over the cave of Abraham had given way, and "a number of Franks had made their entrance therein". And they discovered "(the bodies) of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob", "their shrouds having fallen to pieces, lying propped up against a wall...Then the King, after providing new shrouds, caused the place to be closed once more". Similar information is given in [Ibn at Athir](/wiki/Ali_ibn_al-Athir "Ali ibn al-Athir")'s Chronicle under the year 1119; "In this year was opened the tomb of Abraham, and those of his two sons Isaac and Jacob ...Many people saw the Patriarch. Their limbs had nowise been [disturbed](/wiki/Disturbance_(archaeology) "Disturbance (archaeology)"), and beside them were placed lamps of gold and of silver."[[68]](#cite_note-70) The [Damascene](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") nobleman and historian [Ibn al-Qalanisi](/wiki/Ibn_al-Qalanisi "Ibn al-Qalanisi") in his chronicle also alludes at this time to the discovery of [relics](/wiki/Relics "Relics") purported to be those of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, a discovery that excited eager curiosity among all three communities in Palestine, Muslim, Jewish, and Christian.[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72) Towards the end of the period of Crusader rule, in 1166 [Maimonides](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides") visited Hebron and wrote

> On Sunday, 9 Marheshvan (October 17), I left Jerusalem for Hebron to kiss the tombs of my ancestors in the Cave. On that day, I stood in the cave and prayed, praise be to God, (in gratitude) for everything.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

A royal domain, Hebron was handed over to [Philip of Milly](/wiki/Philip_of_Milly "Philip of Milly") in 1161 and joined with the [Seigneurie of Transjordan](/wiki/Oultrejordain "Oultrejordain"). A bishop was appointed to Hebron in 1168 and the new cathedral church of St Abraham was built in the southern part of the Haram.[[72]](#cite_note-74) In 1167, the [episcopal see of Hebron](/wiki/Hebron_(titular_see) "Hebron (titular see)") was created along with that of [Kerak](/wiki/Kerak "Kerak") and [Sebastia](/wiki/Sebastia,_Nablus "Sebastia, Nablus") (the tomb of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist")).[[73]](#cite_note-75) In 1170, [Benjamin of Tudela](/wiki/Benjamin_of_Tudela "Benjamin of Tudela") visited Hebron, referred to as in its Frankish name *St. Abram de Bron*.[[74]](#cite_note-:9-76) He mentioned the great church called St. Abram, which was once a Jewish place of worship during the time of Muslim rule.[[74]](#cite_note-:9-76) The Gentiles had erected six tombs there, claimed to be those of Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah.[[74]](#cite_note-:9-76) The custodians collected money from pilgrims by presenting these tombs as the tombs of the Patriarchs.[[74]](#cite_note-:9-76) However, if a Jew offered a special reward, they would open an iron gate leading to a series of empty caves, until reaching the third cave where the actual sepulchers of the Patriarchs and Matriarchs were said to be located.[[74]](#cite_note-:9-76)

The Kurdish Muslim [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") retook Hebron in 1187 – again with Jewish assistance according to one late tradition, in exchange for a letter of security allowing them to return to the city and build a synagogue there.[[75]](#cite_note-77) The name of the city was changed back to *Al-Khalil*. A [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurd "Kurd") quarter still existed in the town during the early period of [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") rule.[[76]](#cite_note-78) [Richard the Lionheart](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") retook the city soon after. [Richard of Cornwall](/wiki/Richard,_1st_Earl_of_Cornwall "Richard, 1st Earl of Cornwall"), brought from England to settle the dangerous feuding between [Templars](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar") and [Hospitallers](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller"), whose rivalry imperiled the treaty guaranteeing regional stability stipulated with the Egyptian [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") [As-Salih Ayyub](/wiki/As-Salih_Ayyub "As-Salih Ayyub"), managed to impose peace on the area. But soon after his departure, feuding broke out and in 1241 the Templars mounted a damaging raid on what was, by now, Muslim Hebron, in violation of agreements.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

In 1244, the [Khwarazmians](/wiki/Khwarazmian_dynasty#Mercenaries "Khwarazmian dynasty") destroyed the town, but left the sanctuary untouched.[[51]](#cite_note-Salaville_1910_185-53)

### Mamluk period

In 1260, after [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate_(Cairo) "Mamluk Sultanate (Cairo)") [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") [Baibars](/wiki/Baibars "Baibars") defeated the Mongol army, the [minarets](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") were built onto the sanctuary. Six years later, while on pilgrimage to Hebron, Baibars promulgated an edict forbidding Christians and Jews from entering the sanctuary,[[78]](#cite_note-80) and the climate became less tolerant of Jews and Christians than it had been under the prior [Ayyubid](/wiki/Ayyubid_dynasty "Ayyubid dynasty") rule. The edict for the exclusion of Christians and Jews was not strictly enforced until the middle of the 14th century and by 1490, not even Muslims were permitted to enter the caverns.[[79]](#cite_note-81) The mill at [Artas](/wiki/Artas,_Bethlehem "Artas, Bethlehem") was built in 1307, and the profits from its income were dedicated to the hospital in Hebron.[[80]](#cite_note-82) Between 1318 and 1320, the [Na'ib](/wiki/Na%27ib "Na'ib") of [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") and much of coastal and interior Palestine ordered the construction of [Jawli Mosque](/wiki/Al-Jawali_Mosque "Al-Jawali Mosque") to enlarge the prayer space for worshipers at the Ibrahimi Mosque.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

Hebron was visited by important rabbis over the next two centuries, among them [Nachmanides](/wiki/Nachmanides "Nachmanides") (1270) and [Ishtori HaParchi](/wiki/Ishtori_Haparchi "Ishtori Haparchi") (1322) who noted the [old Jewish cemetery](/wiki/Old_Jewish_cemetery,_Hebron "Old Jewish cemetery, Hebron") there. [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") [imam](/wiki/Imam "Imam") [Ibn Qayyim Al-Jawziyya](/wiki/Ibn_Qayyim_Al-Jawziyya "Ibn Qayyim Al-Jawziyya") (1292–1350) was penalized by the religious authorities in Damascus for refusing to recognize Hebron as a Muslim pilgrimage site, a view also held by his teacher [Ibn Taymiyyah](/wiki/Ibn_Taymiyyah "Ibn Taymiyyah").[[82]](#cite_note-84) The Jewish-Italian traveler, [Meshullam of Volterra](/wiki/Meshullam_of_Volterra "Meshullam of Volterra") (1481) found not more than twenty Jewish families living in Hebron.[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) and recounted how the Jewish women of Hebron would disguise themselves with a veil in order to pass as Muslim women and enter the Cave of the Patriarchs without being recognized as Jews.[[85]](#cite_note-87) Minute descriptions of Hebron were recorded in Stephen von Gumpenberg's Journal (1449), by [Felix Fabri](/wiki/Felix_Fabri "Felix Fabri") (1483) and by [Mejr ed-Din](/wiki/Mejr_ed-Din "Mejr ed-Din")[[86]](#cite_note-88) It was in this period, also, that the [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk") Sultan [Qa'it Bay](/wiki/Qaitbay "Qaitbay") revived the old custom of the Hebron "table of Abraham", and exported it as a model for his own *[madrasa](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa")* in [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina").[[87]](#cite_note-89) This became an immense charitable establishment near the [Haram](/wiki/Haram "Haram"), distributing daily some 1,200 loaves of bread to travelers of all faiths.[[88]](#cite_note-90) The Italian rabbi [Obadiah ben Abraham Bartenura](/wiki/Obadiah_ben_Abraham_Bartenura "Obadiah ben Abraham Bartenura") wrote around 1490:

> I was in the Cave of Machpelah, over which the mosque has been built; and the Arabs hold the place in high honour. All the Kings of the Arabs come here to repeat their prayers, but neither a Jew nor an Arab may enter the Cave itself, where the real graves of the Patriarchs are; the Arabs remain above, and let down burning torches into it through a window, for they keep a light always burning there. Bread and lentil, or some other kind of pulse (seeds of peas or beans), is distributed (by the Muslims) to the poor every day without distinction of faith, and this is done in honour of Abraham.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

### Early Ottoman period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/The_town_of_Hebron._Coloured_lithograph_by_Louis_Haghe_after_Wellcome_V0049466.jpg/250px-The_town_of_Hebron._Coloured_lithograph_by_Louis_Haghe_after_Wellcome_V0049466.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_town_of_Hebron._Coloured_lithograph_by_Louis_Haghe_after_Wellcome_V0049466.jpg)

Hebron in 1839, after a drawing by [David Roberts](/wiki/David_Roberts_(painter) "David Roberts (painter)"), in *[The Holy Land, Syria, Idumea, Arabia, Egypt, and Nubia](/wiki/The_Holy_Land,_Syria,_Idumea,_Arabia,_Egypt,_and_Nubia "The Holy Land, Syria, Idumea, Arabia, Egypt, and Nubia")*

The expansion of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") along the southern Mediterranean coast under sultan [Selim I](/wiki/Selim_I "Selim I") coincided with the establishment of [Inquisition](/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition") commissions by the [Catholic Monarchs](/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs "Catholic Monarchs") in Spain in 1478, which ended centuries of the Iberian *convivencia* (coexistence). The ensuing [expulsions of the Jews](/wiki/Alhambra_Decree "Alhambra Decree") drove many [Sephardi Jews](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") into the Ottoman provinces, and a slow influx of Jews to the Holy Land took place, with notable Sephardi [kabbalists](/wiki/Kabbalah "Kabbalah") settling in Hebron.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen2007xv–xix-92) Over the following two centuries, there was a significant migration of Bedouin tribal groups from the Arabian Peninsula into Palestine. Many settled in three separate villages in the Wādī al-Khalīl, and their descendants later formed the majority of Hebron.[[91]](#cite_note-Büssow_2011_195-93)

The Jewish community fluctuated between eight and ten families throughout the 16th century, and suffered from severe financial straits in the first half of the century.[[92]](#cite_note-94) In 1540, renowned [kabbalist](/wiki/Kabbalist "Kabbalist") [Malkiel Ashkenazi](/wiki/Malkiel_Ashkenazi "Malkiel Ashkenazi") bought a courtyard from the small [Karaite](/wiki/Karaite_Judaism "Karaite Judaism") community, in which he established the Sephardic [Abraham Avinu Synagogue](/wiki/Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue "Abraham Avinu Synagogue").[[93]](#cite_note-95) In 1659, Abraham Pereyra of Amsterdam founded the *Hesed Le'Abraham [yeshiva](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva")* in Hebron, which attracted many students.[[94]](#cite_note-96) In the early 18th century, the Jewish community suffered from heavy debts, almost quadrupling from 1717 to 1729,[[95]](#cite_note-97) and were "almost crushed" from the extortion practiced by the Turkish pashas. In 1773 or 1775, a substantial amount of money was extorted from the Jewish community after a false allegation that the son of a local [sheikh](/wiki/Sheikh "Sheikh") was murdered and thrown into a cesspit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Emissaries](/wiki/Meshulach "Meshulach") from the community were frequently sent overseas to [solicit funds](/wiki/Halukka "Halukka").[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99) During the Ottoman period, the dilapidated patriarchs' tombs were restored to a semblance of dignity.[[98]](#cite_note-100) [Ali Bey](/wiki/Ali_Bey_el_Abbassi "Ali Bey el Abbassi"), in Muslim disguise, was one of the few Westerners to gain access. In 1807 he reported that the sepulchres were covered with carpets of green silk embroidered in gold and those of the wives were covered in red silk.The sultans of Constantinople furnished these carpets, which were renewed from time to time. Ali Bey counted nine, one over the other, on the sepulchre of Abraham.[[99]](#cite_note-101) Hebron also became known for its glass production, based on Bedouin trade networks that brought up minerals from the Dead Sea. The industry is mentioned in travel literature in 19th century written by [Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") travelers to Palestine. [Ulrich Jasper Seetzen](/wiki/Ulrich_Jasper_Seetzen "Ulrich Jasper Seetzen") noted during his travels in Palestine in 1808–09 that 150 persons were employed in the glass industry in Hebron,[[100]](#cite_note-102) based on 26 [kilns](/wiki/Kiln "Kiln").[[101]](#cite_note-103) In 1833, a report on the town in the weekly paper of the London-based [Religious Tract Society](/wiki/Religious_Tract_Society "Religious Tract Society") wrote that Hebron had numerous well-provisioned shops and produced glass lamps which were exported to [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt").[[102]](#cite_note-104) Early 19th-century travelers also noted Hebron's flourishing agriculture. It was a major exporter of *dibse*, grape sugar,[[103]](#cite_note-105) from the famous Dabookeh grapestock characteristic of Hebron.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Frith%2C_Francis_%281822-1898%29_-_Views_in_the_Holy_Land_-_n._428_-_Hebron._Northern_Half_of_the_City_-_recto.jpg/250px-Frith%2C_Francis_%281822-1898%29_-_Views_in_the_Holy_Land_-_n._428_-_Hebron._Northern_Half_of_the_City_-_recto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frith,_Francis_(1822-1898)_-_Views_in_the_Holy_Land_-_n._428_-_Hebron._Northern_Half_of_the_City_-_recto.jpg)

Northern Hebron in the mid-19th century (1850s)

An [Arab peasants' revolt](/wiki/1834_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1834 Arab revolt in Palestine") broke out in April 1834 when [Ibrahim Pasha of Egypt](/wiki/Ibrahim_Pasha_of_Egypt "Ibrahim Pasha of Egypt") announced he would recruit troops from the local Muslim population.[[105]](#cite_note-107) Hebron, headed by its [nazir](/wiki/N%C4%81%E1%BA%93ir "Nāẓir") Abd ar-Rahman Amr, declined to supply its quota of conscripts for the army and suffered badly from the Egyptian campaign to crush the uprising. The town was invested and, when its defenses fell on August 4, it was sacked by Ibrahim Pasha's army.[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-Robinson_88-109)[[108]](#cite_note-110) An estimated 500 Muslims from Hebron were killed in the attack and some 750 were conscripted. 120 youths were abducted and put at the disposal of Egyptian army officers. Most of the Muslim population managed to flee beforehand to the hills. Many Jews fled to Jerusalem, but during the general pillage of the town [at least five were killed](/wiki/1834_Hebron_massacre "1834 Hebron massacre").[[109]](#cite_note-111)
When the government of Ibrahim Pasha fell in 1841, the local clan leader Abd ar-Rahman Amr once again resumed the reins of power as the Sheik of Hebron. Due to his extortionate demands for cash from the local population, most of the Jewish population fled to Jerusalem.[[110]](#cite_note-112) In 1846, the Ottoman Governor-in-chief of Jerusalem (*serasker*), [Kıbrıslı Mehmed Emin Pasha](/wiki/K%C4%B1br%C4%B1sl%C4%B1_Mehmed_Emin_Pasha "Kıbrıslı Mehmed Emin Pasha"), waged a campaign to subdue rebellious sheiks in the Hebron area, and while doing so, allowed his troops to sack the town. Though it was widely rumored that he secretly protected Abd ar-Rahman,[[111]](#cite_note-113) the latter was deported together with other local leaders (such as Muslih al-'Azza of [Bayt Jibrin](/wiki/Bayt_Jibrin "Bayt Jibrin")), but he managed to return to the area in 1848.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

According to Hillel Cohen, the attacks on Jews in this particular period are an exception that proves the rule, that one of the easiest place for Jews to live in the world were in the various countries of the Ottoman Empire. In the mid-eighteenth century, [rabbi Abraham Gershon](/wiki/Abraham_Gershon_of_Kitov "Abraham Gershon of Kitov") of [Kitov](/wiki/Kuty "Kuty") wrote from Hebron that:"the gentiles here very much love the Jews. When there is a *brit milah* (circumcision ceremony) or any other celebration, their most important men come at night and rejoice with the Jews and clap hands and dance with the Jews, just like the Jews'."[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen201515-115)

### Late Ottoman period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Hebron_glass_finished_products_-_Joff_Williams.jpg/250px-Hebron_glass_finished_products_-_Joff_Williams.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron_glass_finished_products_-_Joff_Williams.jpg)

A display of [Hebron glass](/wiki/Hebron_glass "Hebron glass")

By 1850, the Jewish population consisted of 45–60 Sephardic families, some 40 born in the town, and a 30-year-old Ashkenazic community of 50 families, mainly Polish and Russian,[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117) the [Lubavitch Hasidic](/wiki/Chabad "Chabad") movement having established a community in 1823.[[116]](#cite_note-118) The ascendency of Ibrahim Pasha led to a decline in the local glass industry. His plan to build a Mediterranean fleet led to severe logging in Hebron's forests, making firewood for the kilns scarce. At the same time, Egypt began importing cheap European glass. The rerouting of the hajj from Damascus through Transjordan reduced traffic to Hebron, and the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_canal "Suez canal") (1869) precipitated a drop in caravan trade. The consequence was a steady deterioration of the local economy.[[117]](#cite_note-119) At the time, the town was divided into four quarters: the Ancient Quarter (*Harat al-Kadim*) near the Cave of Machpelah; to its south, the Quarter of the Silk Merchant (*Harat al-Kazaz*), inhabited by Jews; the Mamluk-era Sheikh's Quarter (*Harat ash Sheikh*) to the north-west; and further north, the Dense Quarter (*Harat al-Harbah*).[[118]](#cite_note-120)[[119]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/53/Jewish_ghetto_in_hebron%2C_1921.jpg/250px-Jewish_ghetto_in_hebron%2C_1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jewish_ghetto_in_hebron,_1921.jpg)

Jews in Hebron, 1921

In 1855, the newly appointed Ottoman *[pasha](/wiki/Pasha "Pasha")* ("governor") of the *[sanjak](/wiki/Sanjak "Sanjak")* ("district") of Jerusalem, [Kamil Pasha](/wiki/K%C4%B1br%C4%B1sl%C4%B1_Mehmed_Kamil_Pasha "Kıbrıslı Mehmed Kamil Pasha"), attempted to put down a rebellion in the Hebron region. Kamil and his army marched towards Hebron in July 1855, a scene witnessed by representatives of the English, French and other Western consulates. After crushing all opposition, Kamil appointed Salama Amr, brother and rival of Abd al Rachman, as *[nazir](/wiki/N%C4%81%E1%BA%93ir "Nāẓir")* of the Hebron region. Relative quiet reigned in the town for the next 4 years.[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123) In 1866, Hungarian Jews of the [Karlin Hasidic court](/wiki/Karlin_(Hasidic_Dynasty) "Karlin (Hasidic Dynasty)") settled in Hebron.[[122]](#cite_note-Shragai_2008-124) According to [Nadav Shragai](/wiki/Nadav_Shragai "Nadav Shragai"), Arab-Jewish relations were good, and Alter Rivlin, who spoke Arabic and Syrian-Aramaic, was appointed Jewish representative to the city council.[[122]](#cite_note-Shragai_2008-124) During a severe drought in 1869–1871, food in Hebron sold for ten times the normal amount.[[123]](#cite_note-125) From 1874, the Hebron district was administered directly from [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul") as part of the Sanjak of Jerusalem.[[124]](#cite_note-126) By 1874, when [C.R. Conder](/wiki/C._R._Conder "C. R. Conder") visited Hebron under the auspices of the [Palestine Exploration Fund](/wiki/Palestine_Exploration_Fund "Palestine Exploration Fund"), the Jewish community numbered 600 in an overall population of 17,000.[[125]](#cite_note-ConderCR1879-127) The Jews lived in the Quarter of the Corner Gate.[[125]](#cite_note-ConderCR1879-127) In the late 19th century the production of [Hebron glass](/wiki/Hebron_glass "Hebron glass") declined due to competition from imported European glassware, although it continued to be popular among those who could not afford luxury goods and was sold by Jewish merchants.[[126]](#cite_note-128) Glass ornaments from Hebron were exhibited at the [World Fair of 1873 in Vienna](/wiki/Weltausstellung_1873_Wien "Weltausstellung 1873 Wien").

A report from the [consul](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_France,_Jerusalem "Consulate General of France, Jerusalem") of the [French Consulate in Jerusalem](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_France,_Jerusalem "Consulate General of France, Jerusalem") in 1886 suggests that glass-making remained an important source of income for Hebron, with four factories earning 60,000 francs yearly.[[127]](#cite_note-129) While the economy of other cities in Palestine was based on solely on trade, the economy of Hebron was more diverse, including agriculture and livestock herding, along with glassware manufacturing and processing of hides. This was because the most fertile lands were situated within the city limits.[[128]](#cite_note-Taraki_Giacaman-130) Even so, Hebron had an image of being unproductive and an "asylum for the poor and the spiritual".[[129]](#cite_note-Taraki_Giacaman2-131) While the wealthy merchants of Nablus built fine mansions, housing in Hebron consisted of semi-peasant dwellings.[[128]](#cite_note-Taraki_Giacaman-130)

Hebron was described as 'deeply Bedouin and Islamic',[[130]](#cite_note-132) and 'bleakly conservative' in its religious outlook,[[131]](#cite_note-133) with a strong tradition of hostility to Jews.[[132]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-135) It had a reputation for religious zeal in jealously protecting its sites from Jews and Christians, although the Jewish and Christian communities seem to have been an integral part of the local economy.[[91]](#cite_note-Büssow_2011_195-93) As income from commerce declined and tax revenues diminished significantly, the Ottoman government left Hebron to manage its own affairs for the most part, making it "one of the most autonomous regions in late Ottoman Palestine."[[134]](#cite_note-136) The Jewish community was under French protection until 1914. The Jewish presence itself was divided between the traditional Sephardi community, whose members spoke Arabic and adopted Arab dress, and the more recent influx of [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"). They prayed in different synagogues, sent their children to different schools, lived in different quarters and did not intermarry. The community was largely Orthodox and anti-Zionist.[[135]](#cite_note-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138)

### British Mandate period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/British_loyalty_meeting_in_Hebron%2C_3_July_1940.jpg/250px-British_loyalty_meeting_in_Hebron%2C_3_July_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_loyalty_meeting_in_Hebron,_3_July_1940.jpg)

British loyalty meeting in Hebron, July 1940

The British [occupied](/wiki/Occupied_Enemy_Territory_Administration "Occupied Enemy Territory Administration") Hebron on December 8, 1917; governance transited to a [mandate](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") in 1920. Most of Hebron was owned by old Islamic charitable endowments (*[waqfs](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf")*), with about 60% of all the land in and around Hebron belonging to the Tamīm al-Dārī waqf.[[137]](#cite_note-139) During the 1920s, Abd al-Ḥayy al-Khaṭīb was appointed Mufti of Hebron. Before his appointment, he had been a staunch opponent of [Haj Amin](/wiki/Haj_Amin_al-Husseini "Haj Amin al-Husseini"), supported the Muslim National Associations and had good contacts with the Zionists.[[138]](#cite_note-140) Later, al-Khaṭīb became one of the few loyal followers of Haj Amin in Hebron.[[139]](#cite_note-141) During the late Ottoman period, a new ruling elite had emerged in Palestine. They later formed the core of the growing Arab nationalist movement in the early 20th century. During the Mandate period, delegates from Hebron constituted only 1 percent of the political leadership.[[140]](#cite_note-Tarākī_2006_12–14-142) The Palestinian Arab decision to boycott the 1923 elections for a Legislative Council was made at the [fifth Palestinian Congress](/wiki/Palestine_Arab_Congress "Palestine Arab Congress"), after it was reported by Murshid Shahin (an Arab pro-Zionist activist) that there was intense resistance in Hebron to the elections.[[141]](#cite_note-143) Almost no house in Hebron remained undamaged when an [earthquake struck Palestine](/wiki/1927_Jericho_earthquake "1927 Jericho earthquake") on July 11, 1927.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

The Cave of the Patriarchs continued to remain officially closed to non-Muslims, and reports that entry to the site had been relaxed in 1928 were denied by the [Supreme Muslim Council](/wiki/Supreme_Muslim_Council "Supreme Muslim Council").[[143]](#cite_note-145)

At this time following attempts by the [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") government to draft yeshiva students into the army, the Lithuanian [Hebron Yeshiva](/wiki/Hebron_Yeshiva "Hebron Yeshiva") (Knesses Yisroel) relocated to Hebron, after consultations between Rabbi [Nosson Tzvi Finkel](/wiki/Nosson_Tzvi_Finkel_(Slabodka) "Nosson Tzvi Finkel (Slabodka)"), [Yechezkel Sarna](/wiki/Yechezkel_Sarna "Yechezkel Sarna") and [Moshe Mordechai Epstein](/wiki/Moshe_Mordechai_Epstein "Moshe Mordechai Epstein").[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147) and by 1929 had attracted some 265 students from Europe and the United States.[[146]](#cite_note-148) The majority of the Jewish population lived on the outskirts of Hebron along the roads to Be'ersheba and Jerusalem, renting homes owned by Arabs, a number of which were built for the express purpose of housing Jewish tenants, with a few dozen within the city around the synagogues.[[147]](#cite_note-149) During the [1929 Hebron massacre](/wiki/1929_Hebron_massacre "1929 Hebron massacre"), Arab rioters slaughtered some 64 to 67 Jewish men, women and children[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151) and wounded 60, and Jewish homes and synagogues were ransacked; 435 Jews survived by virtue of the shelter and assistance offered them by their Arab neighbors, who hid them.[[150]](#cite_note-152) Some Hebron Arabs, including Ahmad Rashid al-Hirbawi, president of Hebron chamber of commerce, supported the return of Jews after the massacre.[[151]](#cite_note-153) Two years later, 35 families moved back into the ruins of the Jewish quarter, but on the eve of the [Palestinian Arab revolt](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9339_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–39 Arab revolt in Palestine") (April 23, 1936) the British Government decided to move the Jewish community out of Hebron as a precautionary measure to secure its safety. The sole exception was the 8th generation Hebronite Ya'akov ben Shalom Ezra, who processed dairy products in the city, blended in well with its social landscape and resided there under the protection of friends. In November 1947, in anticipation of the [UN partition vote](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine"), the Ezra family closed its shop and left the city.[[152]](#cite_note-154) Yossi Ezra has since tried to regain his family's property through the Israeli courts.[[153]](#cite_note-Levinsohn2011-155)

### Jordanian occupation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Hebron-_1960%27s.jpg/250px-Hebron-_1960%27s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron-_1960%27s.jpg)

Hebron in the 1960s under Jordanian rule

At the beginning of the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), Egypt took control of Hebron. Between May and October, Egypt and Jordan tussled for dominance in Hebron and its environs. Both countries appointed military governors in the town, hoping to gain recognition from Hebron officials. The Egyptians managed to persuade the pro-Jordanian mayor to support their rule, at least superficially, but local opinion turned against them when they imposed taxes. Villagers surrounding Hebron resisted and skirmishes broke out in which some were killed.[[154]](#cite_note-156) By late 1948, part of the Egyptian forces from Bethlehem to Hebron had been cut off from their lines of supply and [Glubb Pasha](/wiki/John_Bagot_Glubb "John Bagot Glubb") sent 350 [Arab Legionnaires](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") and an armored car unit to Hebron to reinforce them there. When the [Armistice](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") was signed, the city thus fell under [Jordanian military control](/wiki/Rule_of_the_West_Bank_and_East_Jerusalem_by_Jordan "Rule of the West Bank and East Jerusalem by Jordan"). The armistice agreement between Israel with Jordan intended to allow Israeli Jewish pilgrims to visit Hebron, but, as Jews of all nationalities were forbidden by Jordan into the country, this did not occur.[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)

In December 1948, the [Jericho Conference](/wiki/Jericho_Conference "Jericho Conference"), held by Jordan, was convened to decide the future of the West Bank. Hebron notables, headed by mayor [Muhamad 'Ali al-Ja'bari](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Ja%27abari "Muhammad Ali Ja'abari"), voted in favor of becoming part of [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and to recognize [Abdullah I of Jordan](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan") as their king. The subsequent unilateral annexation benefited the Arabs of Hebron, who during the 1950s, played a significant role in the economic development of Jordan.[[157]](#cite_note-159)[[158]](#cite_note-160)

Although a significant number of people relocated to Jerusalem from Hebron during the Jordanian period,[[159]](#cite_note-161) Hebron itself saw a considerable increase in population with 35,000 settling in the town.[[160]](#cite_note-Efrat_1984_192-162) During this period, signs of the previous Jewish presence in Hebron were removed.[[161]](#cite_note-163)

### Israeli period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Dan_Hadani_collection_%28990044355400205171%29.jpg/250px-Dan_Hadani_collection_%28990044355400205171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dan_Hadani_collection_(990044355400205171).jpg)

Israeli soldiers enforcing the curfew, 1969

After the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") in June 1967, Israel [occupied](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") Hebron along with the rest of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), establishing a [military government](/wiki/Israeli_Military_Governorate "Israeli Military Governorate") to rule the area. In an attempt to reach a [land for peace](/wiki/Land_for_peace "Land for peace") deal, [Yigal Allon](/wiki/Yigal_Allon "Yigal Allon") proposed that Israel annex 45% of the West Bank and return the remainder to Jordan.[[162]](#cite_note-164) According to the [Allon Plan](/wiki/Allon_Plan "Allon Plan"), the city of Hebron would lie in Jordanian territory, and in order to determine Israel's own border, Allon suggested building a Jewish settlement adjacent to Hebron.[[163]](#cite_note-165) [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") also considered that Hebron was the one sector of the conquered territories that should remain under Jewish control and be open to Jewish settlement.[[164]](#cite_note-166) Apart from its symbolic message to the international community that Israel's rights in Hebron were, according to Jews, inalienable,[[165]](#cite_note-167) settling Hebron also had theological significance in some quarters.[[166]](#cite_note-168) For some, the capture of Hebron by Israel had unleashed a messianic fervor.[[167]](#cite_note-169)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Hebron105.JPG/250px-Hebron105.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hebron105.JPG)

Constructed in 1893, this former Jewish clinic in central Hebron now forms part of an Israeli settlement.

Survivors and descendants of the prior community are mixed. Some support the project of Jewish redevelopment, others commend living in peace with Hebronite Arabs, while a third group recommend a full pullout.[[168]](#cite_note-jpt-170) Descendants supporting the latter views have met with Palestinian leaders in Hebron.[[169]](#cite_note-agf-171) In 1997 one group of descendants dissociated themselves from the settlers by calling them an obstacle to peace.[[169]](#cite_note-agf-171) On May 15, 2006, a member of a group who is a direct descendant of the 1929 refugees[[170]](#cite_note-shragai-172) urged the government to continue its support of Jewish settlement, and allow the return of eight families evacuated the previous January from homes they set up in emptied shops near the Avraham Avinu neighborhood.[[168]](#cite_note-jpt-170) [Beit HaShalom](/wiki/Beit_HaShalom "Beit HaShalom"), established in 2007 under disputed circumstances, was under court orders permitting its forced evacuation.[[171]](#cite_note-173)[[172]](#cite_note-174)[[173]](#cite_note-175)[[174]](#cite_note-176) All the Jewish settlers were expelled on December 3, 2008.[[175]](#cite_note-177)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Israeli_soldiers_on_Palestine_street.jpg/250px-Israeli_soldiers_on_Palestine_street.jpg)](/wiki/File:Israeli_soldiers_on_Palestine_street.jpg)

Israeli soldiers patrol an open-air market.

Immediately after the 1967 war, mayor al-Ja'bari had unsuccessfully promoted the creation of an autonomous Palestinian entity in the West Bank, and by 1972, he was advocating for a confederal arrangement with Jordan instead. al-Ja'bari nevertheless consistently fostered a conciliatory policy towards Israel.[[176]](#cite_note-178) He was ousted by Fahad Qawasimi in the 1976 mayoral election, which marked a shift in support towards pro-PLO nationalist leaders.[[177]](#cite_note-179) Supporters of Jewish settlement within Hebron see their program as the reclamation of an important heritage dating back to Biblical times, which was dispersed or, it is argued, stolen by Arabs after the massacre of 1929.[[178]](#cite_note-180)[[179]](#cite_note-181) The purpose of settlement is to return to the 'land of our forefathers',[[180]](#cite_note-182) and the Hebron model of reclaiming sacred sites in Palestinian territories has pioneered a pattern for settlers in Bethlehem and Nablus.[[181]](#cite_note-183) Many reports, foreign and Israeli, are sharply critical of the behavior of Hebronite settlers.[[182]](#cite_note-184)[[183]](#cite_note-185)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Hebron.jpg/500px-2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Hebron.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_OCHA_OpT_map_Hebron.jpg)

2018 [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_for_the_Coordination_of_Humanitarian_Affairs "United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs") map of the area, showing the [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") arrangements.

Sheik Farid Khader heads the Ja'bari tribe, consisting of some 35,000 people, which is considered one of the most important tribes in Hebron. For years, members of the Ja'bari tribe were the mayors of Hebron. Khader regularly meets with settlers and Israeli government officials and is a strong opponent of both the concept of Palestinian State and the Palestinian Authority itself. Khader believes that Jews and Arabs must learn to coexist.[[184]](#cite_note-186) [A violent episode](/wiki/1980_Hebron_attack "1980 Hebron attack") occurred May 2, 1980, when an [Al Fatah](/wiki/Al_Fatah "Al Fatah") squad killed five yeshiva students and one other person on their way home from Sabbath prayer at the [Tomb of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Patriarchs "Tomb of the Patriarchs").[[185]](#cite_note-187) The event provided a major motivation for settlers near Hebron to join the [Jewish Underground](/wiki/Jewish_Underground "Jewish Underground").[[186]](#cite_note-188)

In the 1980s Hebron, became the center of the Jewish [Kach](/wiki/Kach_and_Kahane_Chai "Kach and Kahane Chai") movement, a designated terrorist organization,[[187]](#cite_note-189) whose first operations started there, and provided a model for similar behavior in other settlements. On July 26, 1983, Israeli settlers [attacked](/wiki/Attack_on_students_at_the_Islamic_College_in_Hebron "Attack on students at the Islamic College in Hebron") the Islamic University and shot three people dead and injured over thirty others.[[188]](#cite_note-190) The 1994 [Shamgar Commission](/wiki/Shamgar_Commission "Shamgar Commission") of Inquiry concluded that Israeli authorities had consistently failed to investigate or prosecute crimes committed by settlers against Palestinians. Hebron IDF commander Noam Tivon said that his foremost concern is to "ensure the security of the Jewish settlers" and that Israeli "soldiers have acted with the utmost restraint and have not initiated any shooting attacks or violence".[[189]](#cite_note-191)

#### Oslo Accords

Main article: [Israeli–Palestinian conflict in Hebron](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict_in_Hebron "Israeli–Palestinian conflict in Hebron")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/HebronNet.jpg/250px-HebronNet.jpg)](/wiki/File:HebronNet.jpg)

A net in the streets of the old city to protect Palestinians from the garbage thrown by Israeli settlers on the upper floors, June 2010[[190]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Al_Khalil_Hebron_%28135152845%29.jpeg/250px-Al_Khalil_Hebron_%28135152845%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Al_Khalil_Hebron_(135152845).jpeg)

An Israeli checkpoint in Hebron in 2015

Hebron was the one city excluded from the interim agreement of September 1995 to restore rule over all Palestinian West Bank cities to the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority").[[191]](#cite_note-Kimmerling_2003_443-193) IDF soldiers see their job as being to protect Israeli settlers from Palestinian residents, not to police the Israeli settlers. IDF soldiers are instructed to leave violent Israeli settlers for the police to deal with.[[192]](#cite_note-194)[[193]](#cite_note-195) Since The [Oslo Agreement](/wiki/Oslo_Agreement "Oslo Agreement"), violent episodes have been recurrent in the city. The [Cave of the Patriarchs massacre](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs_massacre "Cave of the Patriarchs massacre") took place on February 25, 1994, when [Baruch Goldstein](/wiki/Baruch_Goldstein "Baruch Goldstein"), an Israeli physician and resident of [Kiryat Arba](/wiki/Kiryat_Arba "Kiryat Arba"), opened fire on Muslims at prayer in the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs"), killing 29, and wounding 125 before the survivors overcame and killed him.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Standing orders for Israeli soldiers on duty in Hebron disallowed them from firing on fellow Jews, even if they were shooting Arabs.[[195]](#cite_note-197) This event was condemned by the Israeli Government, and the extreme right-wing [Kach](/wiki/Kach_and_Kahane_Chai "Kach and Kahane Chai") party was banned as a result.[[196]](#cite_note-198) The Israeli government also tightened restrictions on the movement of Palestinians in H2, closed their vegetable and meat markets, and banned Palestinian cars on Al-Shuhada Street.[[197]](#cite_note-199) The park near the Cave of the Patriarchs for recreation and barbecues is off-limits for Arab Hebronites.[[198]](#cite_note-200) Following the 1995 [Oslo Agreement](/wiki/Oslo_Agreement "Oslo Agreement") and subsequent 1997 [Hebron Agreement](/wiki/Hebron_Agreement "Hebron Agreement"), Palestinian cities were placed under the exclusive jurisdiction of the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority"), with the exception of Hebron,[[199]](#cite_note-Alimi_2013_178-201) which was split into two sectors: H1 is controlled by the Palestinian Authority and H2 – which includes the [Old City of Hebron](/wiki/Old_City_of_Hebron "Old City of Hebron") – remained under the military control of Israel.[[191]](#cite_note-Kimmerling_2003_443-193)[[200]](#cite_note-202) Around 120,000 Palestinians live in H1, while around 30,000 Palestinians along with around 700 Israelis remain under Israeli military control in H2. As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hebron&action=edit), a total of 86 Jewish families lived in Hebron.[[201]](#cite_note-203) The IDF ([Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces")) may not enter H1 unless under Palestinian escort. Palestinians cannot approach areas where settlers live without special permits from the IDF.[[202]](#cite_note-hebron-wp-204) The Jewish settlement is widely considered to be illegal by the international community, although the Israeli government disputes this.[[203]](#cite_note-BBC_GC4-205)

Over the period of the [First Intifada](/wiki/First_Intifada "First Intifada") and [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada"), the Jewish community was subjected to attacks by Palestinian militants, especially during the periods of the intifadas; which saw 3 fatal stabbings and 9 fatal shootings in between the first and [second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") (0.9% of all fatalities in Israel and the West Bank) and 17 fatal shootings (9 soldiers and 8 settlers) and 2 fatalities from a bombing during the second Intifada,[[204]](#cite_note-mfa.gov.il2-206) and thousands of rounds fired on it from the hills above the Abu-Sneina and Harat al-Sheikh neighborhoods. On November 15, 2002, 12 Israeli soldiers were killed (Hebron Brigade commander Colonel [Dror Weinberg](/wiki/Dror_Weinberg "Dror Weinberg") and two other officers, 6 soldiers and 3 members of the security unit of Kiryat Arba) in [an ambush](/wiki/2002_Hebron_ambush "2002 Hebron ambush").[[205]](#cite_note-207) Two [Temporary International Presence in Hebron](/wiki/Temporary_International_Presence_in_Hebron "Temporary International Presence in Hebron") observers were killed by Palestinian gunmen in a shooting attack on the road to Hebron[[206]](#cite_note-208)[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210) On March 27, 2001, a Palestinian sniper targeted and killed the Jewish baby [Shalhevet Pass](/wiki/Murder_of_Shalhevet_Pass "Murder of Shalhevet Pass"). The sniper was caught in 2022.[[209]](#cite_note-211) Hebron is one of the three West Bank towns from which the majority of suicide bombers originate. In May 2003, three students of the Hebron Polytechnic University carried out three separate suicide attacks.[[210]](#cite_note-212) In August 2003, in what both Islamic groups described as a retaliation, a 29-year-old preacher from Hebron, Raed Abdel-Hamed Mesk, broke a unilateral Palestinian ceasefire by killing 23 and injured over 130 in a [bus bombing](/wiki/Shmuel_HaNavi_bus_bombing "Shmuel HaNavi bus bombing") in Jerusalem.[[211]](#cite_note-213)[[212]](#cite_note-214) In 2007, the Palestinian population in H2 declined due to Israeli security measures such as extended curfews, strict restrictions on movement,[[213]](#cite_note-215) the closure of Palestinian businesses and settler harassment.[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[217]](#cite_note-btselem2007-219) Palestinians are barred from using [Al-Shuhada Street](/wiki/Al-Shuhada_Street "Al-Shuhada Street"), a principal commercial thoroughfare that is locally nicknamed "Apartheid Street" as a result.[[202]](#cite_note-hebron-wp-204)[[218]](#cite_note-220)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Al_Khalil_Hebron_%28135198889%29.jpeg/250px-Al_Khalil_Hebron_%28135198889%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Al_Khalil_Hebron_(135198889).jpeg)

The Israeli settlement of Beit Romano, in the old town, 2015

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/SettlersShuhadaStreet.jpg/250px-SettlersShuhadaStreet.jpg)](/wiki/File:SettlersShuhadaStreet.jpg)

Armed Israeli settlers on [Al-Shuhada Street](/wiki/Al-Shuhada_Street "Al-Shuhada Street"), November 6, 2010

Israeli organization [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem") states that there have been "grave violations" of Palestinian human rights in Hebron because of the "presence of the settlers within the city". The organization cites regular incidents of "almost daily physical violence and property damage by settlers in the city", curfews and restrictions of movement that are "among the harshest in the Occupied Territories", and violence by Israeli border policemen and the IDF against Palestinians who live in the city's H2 sector.[[219]](#cite_note-221)[[220]](#cite_note-222)[[221]](#cite_note-223) According to [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"), Palestinian areas of Hebron are frequently subject to indiscriminate firing by the IDF, leading to many casualties.[[222]](#cite_note-HRW-storm2-224) One former IDF soldier, with experience in policing Hebron, has testified to [Breaking the Silence](/wiki/Breaking_the_Silence_(non-governmental_organization) "Breaking the Silence (non-governmental organization)"), that on the briefing wall of his unit a sign describing their mission aim was hung that read: "To disrupt the routine of the inhabitants of the neighbourhood."[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreedland201222-225) Hebron mayor [Mustafa Abdel Nabi](/w/index.php?title=Mustafa_Abdel_Nabi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mustafa Abdel Nabi (page does not exist)") invited the [Christian Peacemaker Teams](/wiki/Christian_Peacemaker_Teams "Christian Peacemaker Teams") to assist the local Palestinian community in opposition to what they describe as Israeli military occupation, collective punishment, settler harassment, [home demolitions](/wiki/House_demolition_in_the_Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "House demolition in the Israeli–Palestinian conflict") and land expropriation.[[224]](#cite_note-226) In 2017, [Temporary International Presence in Hebron](/wiki/Temporary_International_Presence_in_Hebron "Temporary International Presence in Hebron") (TIPH) issued a confidential report covering their 20 years of work in Hebron. The report, based in part on over 40,000 incidents reported during this period, stated that Israel violated international law in Hebron and has breached the rights of residents as established by the [International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights](/wiki/International_Covenant_on_Civil_and_Political_Rights "International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights"). The report claimed that Israel violated Article 49 of the [Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention"), which prohibits the deportation of civilians from occupied territory. Israeli settlement in Hebron was also cited as a violation.[[225]](#cite_note-20yrs-227)

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Grapes_in_Hebron.jpg/250px-Grapes_in_Hebron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grapes_in_Hebron.jpg)

Grape farming in Hebron

Hebron is situated on the southern [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank").[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) Nestled in the [Judaean Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains"), it lies 930 meters (3,050 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) Hebron is located 30 kilometers (19 mi) south of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), 60.1 kilometers (37.3 mi) east of [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), 43 kilometers (27 mi) northeast of [Beersheba](/wiki/Beersheba "Beersheba") and 68.4 kilometers (42.5 mi) southeast of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), both in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and 89.8 kilometers (55.8 mi) northeast of [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan").[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) The city is surrounded by [Bani Na'im](/wiki/Bani_Na%27im "Bani Na'im") and [Dura](/wiki/Dura,_Hebron "Dura, Hebron") to the east, [Halhul](/wiki/Halhul "Halhul") to the north, [Taffuh](/wiki/Taffuh "Taffuh") to the west and [Yatta](/wiki/Yatta,_Hebron "Yatta, Hebron") to the south.[[227]](#cite_note-:15-229)[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) The Israeli–controlled H2 region is located in the eastern region of the city.[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228)

It is one of the highest cities in the area and was, until the 19th century, considered the highest city in the Middle East.[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) With the governorate and metropolitan area, it forms about 19% of the West Bank total area.[[228]](#cite_note-230) The city is surrounded by several mountains and hills, including the [Hebron Hills](/wiki/Hebron_Hills "Hebron Hills") (*Jabal al–Khalil*) and [Mount Nabi Yunis](/wiki/Mount_Nabi_Yunis,_Palestine "Mount Nabi Yunis, Palestine").[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228)[[229]](#cite_note-231) The Mount Nabi Yunis, situated north of the city, is the highest point in Palestine, with an altitude of 1,030 meters (3,380 ft).[[230]](#cite_note-232) While the Hebron Hills is southern part of the wider [Judaean Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains"), which spreads throughout Israel and Palestine and have an altitude of 1,026 m (3,366 ft).[[231]](#cite_note-google1-233) The two larger settlements whose population exceeds 7000 sit on the hills overlooking the Hebron's eastern quarters – [Kirayt Arba](/wiki/Kiryat_Arba "Kiryat Arba") and [Givat Harsina](/wiki/Givat_Harsina "Givat Harsina").[[232]](#cite_note-234) Wadi al–Quff near Hebron is one of the largest natural reserves in Palestine.[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235) Located towards northeast of the city, it is surrounded by nearby towns and villages of [Tarqumia](/wiki/Tarqumiyah "Tarqumiyah"), Halhoul, [Beit Kahel](/wiki/Beit_Kahil "Beit Kahil") and [Beit Ola](/wiki/Beit_Ula "Beit Ula").[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235) The natural reserve covers up an area of 3.73 square kilometers (920 acres).[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235) Wadi al–Quff Natural reserve is home to some of the rare species of animals and plants.[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235)

Hebron is located on fertile mountainous area, making the city agriculturally rich, thus giving it a strategic importance.[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) This is the reason for Hebron, today being a hub for cultivation of fruits and vineyards.[[234]](#cite_note-236) The alternative sources of water network is cisterns.[[227]](#cite_note-:15-229) There are ten springs and three wells in the city.[[227]](#cite_note-:15-229) The water of springs and wells are not currently used.[[227]](#cite_note-:15-229)[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) The [Hebron River](/wiki/Nahal_Hevron "Nahal Hevron") (*Wadi al–Khalil*), known as *Nahal Hebron* in Hebrew located along the region of [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea") and [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"), is one of the water sources for the city.[[235]](#cite_note-Eco-237)[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228) Currently the river is polluted, mainly due to the generation of waste, mostly by the industrial areas, situated on the city's east and south.[[235]](#cite_note-Eco-237)[[226]](#cite_note-:13-228)

### Climate

The climate in Hebron is temperate and the mean year-round temperature ranges between 15 and 16° (an average of 7° in winter and 21° in summer).[[236]](#cite_note-:14-238) Annual precipitations average around 502 mm.[[236]](#cite_note-:14-238) Hebron has *a* Mediterranean, hot summer climate (Classification: Csa).[[236]](#cite_note-:14-238) The city's yearly temperature is 22.74 °C (72.93 °F) and it is 0.14% higher than Palestine's averages.[[236]](#cite_note-:14-238) It typically receives about 15.72 millimeters (0.62 inches) of precipitation and has 39.47 rainy days (10.81% of the time) annually, during January and February.[[236]](#cite_note-:14-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239)
