### Urban development

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/King_faisal_street.jpg/250px-King_faisal_street.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_faisal_street.jpg)

King Faisal Street in Hebron was named in commemoration of [Shah Faisal](/wiki/Shah_Faisal "Shah Faisal"), the [king of Saudi Arabia](/wiki/King_of_Saudi_Arabia "King of Saudi Arabia") from 1964 to 1975

Historically, the city consisted of four densely populated quarters: the *[suq](/wiki/Suq "Suq")* and *Harat al-Masharqa* adjacent to the [Ibrahimi Mosque](/wiki/Ibrahimi_Mosque "Ibrahimi Mosque"), the Silk Merchant Quarter (*Haret Kheitun*) to the south and the Sheikh Quarter (*Haret al-Sheikh*) to the north.[[239]](#cite_note-:11-241) It is believed the basic urban structure of the city had been established by the Mamluk period, during which time the city also had Jewish, Christian and Kurdish quarters.[[240]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_235–236-242)
In the mid 19th-century, Hebron was still divided into four quarters, but the Christian quarter had disappeared.[[240]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_235–236-242) The sections included the ancient quarter surrounding the [Cave of Machpelah](/wiki/Cave_of_Machpelah "Cave of Machpelah"), the *Haret Kheitun* (the Jewish Quarter, *Haret el-Yahud*), the *Haret el-Sheikh* and the [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") Quarter.[[241]](#cite_note-books.google.co.uk-243) As Hebron's population gradually increased, inhabitants preferred to build upwards rather than leave the safety of their neighborhoods.[[239]](#cite_note-:11-241) By the 1880s, better security provided by the Ottoman authorities allowed the town to expand and a new commercial center, *Bab el-Zawiye*, emerged.[[242]](#cite_note-244) As development continued, new spacious and taller structures were built to the north-west.[[243]](#cite_note-245) In 1918, the town consisted of dense clusters of residential dwellings along the valley, rising onto the slopes above it.[[244]](#cite_note-Kedar_2000_112–113-246) By the 1920s, the town was made up of seven quarters: *el-Sheikh* and *Bab el-Zawiye* to the west, *el-Kazzazin*, *el-Akkabi* and *el-Haram* in the center, *el-Musharika* to the south and *el-Kheitun* in the east.[[245]](#cite_note-247) Urban sprawl had spread onto the surrounding hills by 1945.[[244]](#cite_note-Kedar_2000_112–113-246)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/%D8%B3%D9%85%D8%A7%D8%A1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg/250px-%D8%B3%D9%85%D8%A7%D8%A1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D8%B3%D9%85%D8%A7%D8%A1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg)

Sunset in the sky of Hebron Ras al-Jura

The large population increase under Jordanian rule resulted in about 1,800 new houses being built, most of them along the [Hebron-Jerusalem highway](/wiki/Jerusalem_Road_21 "Jerusalem Road 21"), stretching northwards for over 3 miles (5 km) at a depth of 600 ft (200m) either way. Some 500 houses were built elsewhere on surrounding rural land. There was less development to the south-east, where housing units extended along the valley for about 1 mile (1.5 km).[[160]](#cite_note-Efrat_1984_192-162) In 1971, with the assistance of the Israeli and Jordanian governments, the [Hebron University](/wiki/Hebron_University "Hebron University"), an Islamic university, was founded.[[246]](#cite_note-248)[[247]](#cite_note-249) In an attempt to enhance the view of the [Ibrahami Mosque](/wiki/Ibrahimi_Mosque "Ibrahimi Mosque"), Jordan demolished whole blocks of ancient houses opposite its entrance, which also resulted in improved access to the historic site.[[248]](#cite_note-250) The Jordanians also demolished the old synagogue located in the el-Kazzazin Quarter. In 1976, Israel recovered the site, which had been converted into an animal pen, and by 1989, a settler courtyard had been established there.[[249]](#cite_note-251)

Today, the area along the north–south axis to the east comprises the modern city of Hebron (also called Upper Hebron, *Khalil Foq*).[[250]](#cite_note-:12-252) It was established towards the end of the Ottoman period, its inhabitants being upper and middle class Hebronites who moved there from the crowded old city, *Balde al-Qadime* (also called Lower Hebron, *Khalil Takht*).[[251]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_230–233-253) The northern part of Upper Hebron includes some up-scale residential districts and also houses the Hebron University, private hospitals and the only two luxury hotels in the city.[[250]](#cite_note-:12-252) The main commercial artery of the city is located here, situated along the [Jerusalem Road](/wiki/Jerusalem_Road_20 "Jerusalem Road 20"), and includes modern multi-storey shopping malls.[[250]](#cite_note-:12-252) Also in this area are villas and apartment complexes built on the *krum*, rural lands and vineyards, which used to function as recreation areas during the summer months until the early Jordanian period.[[251]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_230–233-253) The southern part is where the working-class neighborhoods are located, along with large industrial zones and the [Hebron Polytechnic University](/wiki/Palestine_Polytechnic_University "Palestine Polytechnic University").[[251]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_230–233-253) The main municipal and governmental buildings are located in the center of the city.[[250]](#cite_note-:12-252) This area includes high-rise concrete and glass developments and also some distinct Ottoman era one-storey family houses, adorned with arched entrances, decorative motifs and ironwork. Hebron's domestic appliance and textile markets are located here along two parallel roads that lead to the entrance of the old city.[[251]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_230–233-253) Many of these have been relocated from the old commercial center of the city, known as the vegetable market (*hesbe*), which was closed down by the Israeli military during the 1990s.[[250]](#cite_note-:12-252) The vegetable market is now located in the square of *Bab el-Zawiye*.[[251]](#cite_note-De_Cesari_2009_230–233-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Hebron_city_banner.JPG/1280px-Hebron_city_banner.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hebron_city_banner.JPG "Panoramic view of residential area of Hebron")

Panoramic view of residential area of Hebron

Hebron has, historically, been an Arab city but a small Jewish community has existed since the 16th century,[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYiftachelRoded2010193-259) encouraged to settle there by the Ottoman authorities following the [expulsion of Jews from Spain](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Jews_from_Spain "Expulsion of Jews from Spain").[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeberDubovoy1995337-260) In 1820, it was reported that there were about 1,000 Jews in Hebron.[[259]](#cite_note-Turnerp261-261) In 1838, the total population was estimated at 10,000, including an estimated 1,500 taxable Muslim households, and to 41 Jewish taxpayers. Taxpayers consisted here of male heads of households who owned even a very small shop or piece of land. Two hundred Jews and one Christian household were under 'European protections'.[[107]](#cite_note-Robinson_88-109) In 1842, it was estimated that about 400 Arab and 120 Jewish families lived in Hebron, the Jews having been diminished in number following the destruction of 1834.[[260]](#cite_note-PackardUnion1842-262) Its population stood at 16,577 in 1922 of which 16,074 (97%) were Muslim, 430 (2.5%) were Jewish, and 73 (0.4%) were Christian.[[252]](#cite_note-barron-254)[[261]](#cite_note-CP7fYghBFQC_1936,_p._887-263)

The Jewish community was expelled from the city following the massacre of nearly 70 Jews in 1929. However, since Israel gained control of the West Bank in 1967, Hebron and eastern Jerusalem have been the only cities in the region to receive [Jewish settlement](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement").[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYiftachelRoded2010193-259)

Hebron had a population of 201,063 [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") in 2017,[[3]](#cite_note-PrelimCensus2017-3) and seven hundred Jewish settlers concentrated on the outskirts of its [Old City](/wiki/Old_City_of_Hebron "Old City of Hebron"). Roughly 20% of the city, including 35,000 Palestinians, under Israeli military administration, lives in the region of H2 Hebron.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeuman20184-264) The surrounding area forms the Hebron metropolitan area, with an estimated population of around 782,227 as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hebron&action=edit).[[263]](#cite_note-PNIC-265) It is third largest metropolitan area in Palestine, after Gaza and Jerusalem.[[263]](#cite_note-PNIC-265)

Hebron is also home to several ethnic minority and foreign diaspora communities.[[264]](#cite_note-KD-266) [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") have been living in the city since [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin")'s [conquest of Palestine](/wiki/Battle_of_Hattin "Battle of Hattin").[[264]](#cite_note-KD-266) Along with Jerusalem and Gaza, the city is also home to [Palestinians of Kurdish descent](/wiki/Kurds_in_Palestine "Kurds in Palestine").[[264]](#cite_note-KD-266) Nearly a third of the population of Hebron is considered to be of Kurdish background.[[264]](#cite_note-KD-266) The Kurdish Quarter, known as *Harat al-Akrad*, still exists today.[[264]](#cite_note-KD-266) Hebron is also home to a small Samaritan community, after Nablus.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

## Economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Palestinian_keffiyeh_loom.jpg/250px-Palestinian_keffiyeh_loom.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palestinian_keffiyeh_loom.jpg)

A loom at work making keffiyeh at the Hirbawi factory in Hebron.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Hebron216.JPG/250px-Hebron216.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hebron216.JPG)

Industrial area of Hebron

Hebron is a leading commercial and industrial center in the Levantine region.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) The presence of [minerals and resources](/wiki/Energy_in_Palestine "Energy in Palestine") in surroundings have increased the city's value.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) It emerged as in important trade hub in the West Bank.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Hebron is most productive region in the country after [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem")–[Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem")–[Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah") area. The H1 Area, which is under control of [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") have been a large contributor to the city's economy.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Despite having tense relations, Israelis and Palestinians have strong trade relations in Hebron.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) The city is popular for its ceramics and glass industry.[[267]](#cite_note-269)

It is the source of 60% of stone and marble resources in the West Bank.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) 33% of the Palestine's [GNP](/wiki/GNP "GNP") is from Hebron, including 60% of the jewelry industry and jewelry production, 28% of the output in the agricultural sector and 75% of the leather and shoe industry.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Most agricultural products from Palestinian controlled Hebron are sent to Israel.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Trade volume between Israel and the Palestine reaches $30 billion annually and the city trades with [China](/wiki/China "China") as well.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) The minimum wage is 50 [NIS](/wiki/Israeli_new_shekel "Israeli new shekel") per day versus an average of 30 NIS per day in other Palestinian areas.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Hebron_CBD_night_1.jpg/250px-Hebron_CBD_night_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron_CBD_night_1.jpg)

Ein Sarah Street, Hebron Central Business District at night

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Inside_Hebron_Mall.jpg/250px-Inside_Hebron_Mall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_Hebron_Mall.jpg)

Inside the Hebron City Center Mall

From the 1970s to the early 1990s, a third of those who lived in the city worked in the shoe industry. According to the shoe factory owner Tareq Abu Felat, the number reached least 35,000 people and there were more than 1,000 workshops around the city.[[268]](#cite_note-AJShoe-270) Statistics from the Chamber of Commerce in Hebron put the figure at 40,000 people employed in 1,200 shoe businesses.[[269]](#cite_note-AMShoe-271) However, the [1993 Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") and [1994 Paris Protocol](/wiki/Protocol_on_Economic_Relations "Protocol on Economic Relations") between Israel and the [PLO](/wiki/PLO "PLO") made it possible to mass import Chinese goods as the Palestinian National Authority, which was created after the Oslo Accords, did not regulate it. They later put import taxes but the Abu Felat, who also is the Palestinian Federation of Leather Industries's chairman, said more is still needed.[[268]](#cite_note-AJShoe-270) The [Palestinian government](/wiki/Palestinian_government "Palestinian government") decided to impose an additional tax of 35% on products from China from April 2013.[[269]](#cite_note-AMShoe-271) Ninety percent of the shoes in Palestine are now estimated to come from China, which Palestinian industry workers say are of much lower quality but also much cheaper,[[268]](#cite_note-AJShoe-270) and the Chinese are more aesthetic. Another factor contributing to the decline of the local industry is [Israeli restrictions](/wiki/Palestinian_freedom_of_movement "Palestinian freedom of movement") on Palestinian exports.[[269]](#cite_note-AMShoe-271) Today, there are less than 300 workshops in the shoe industry, who only run part-time, and they employ around 3,000–4,000 people. More than 50% of the shoes are exported to Israel, where consumers have a better economy. Less than 25% goes to the Palestinian market, with some going to [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and other [Arab countries](/wiki/Arab_countries "Arab countries").[[268]](#cite_note-AJShoe-270)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/%D8%B2%D8%AC%D8%A7%D8%AC_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg/250px-%D8%B2%D8%AC%D8%A7%D8%AC_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D8%B2%D8%AC%D8%A7%D8%AC_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AE%D9%84%D9%8A%D9%84.jpg)

[Hebron glass](/wiki/Hebron_glass "Hebron glass") in a market

The most advanced printing press in the Middle East is in Hebron.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Hebron is major source of import goods to Israel.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Mattresses manufactured in Hebron are exported to Israeli markets in [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), [Beersheba](/wiki/Beersheba "Beersheba") and [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa").[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Around 17,000 factories and workshops are located throughout the Area H1.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) Historically, the traditional glass industry is popular in Hebron.[[266]](#cite_note-:4-268) A new industrial city has been built in [Tarqumiyah](/wiki/Tarqumiyah "Tarqumiyah"), which houses more than 140 factories. Royal Industrial Trading operates a pipe manufacturing plant in Hebron, which is spread across an area of 40,000 square meters (9.9 acres) and employs over 650 people.[[270]](#cite_note-272) In 2021, an electronic recycling factory was opened in [Idhna](/wiki/Idna "Idna") and operates to this day.[[271]](#cite_note-273) The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") proposed to construct a regional water treatment plant, which will treat existing sewage stream coming from 80% of the city.[[272]](#cite_note-274) The city is a hub for the jewelry industry and houses approximately 70 jewelry factories employing over 1500 workers.[[273]](#cite_note-275)

Super Nimer company manufactures sanitary ware products and water network from its factory, whose area ranges from 30,000 square meters (7.4 acres) to 45,000 square meters (11 acres).[[274]](#cite_note-:5-276) Opened in 2004, Super Tiger operates a factory spread across an area of 7 acres (28,000 m2).[[274]](#cite_note-:5-276) During the [COVID-19 pandemic in the State of Palestine](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_State_of_Palestine "COVID-19 pandemic in the State of Palestine"), Hebron rapidly transformed into a medical supplies manufacturing hub, with numerous factories installing and commissioning new production lines for the product and was approved by the [Ministry of National Economy](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Economy_(Palestine) "Ministry of National Economy (Palestine)").[[275]](#cite_note-277)

## Political status

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Hebron_Redeployment_Map.jpg/250px-Hebron_Redeployment_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron_Redeployment_Map.jpg)

Official 1997 agreement map of Palestinian controlled H1 and Israeli controlled H2.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Hebron_redeployment_1997.jpg/250px-Hebron_redeployment_1997.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron_redeployment_1997.jpg)

Illustration showing areas H1 and H2 and adjacent Israeli settlements

1997 [Protocol Concerning the Redeployment in Hebron](/wiki/Protocol_Concerning_the_Redeployment_in_Hebron "Protocol Concerning the Redeployment in Hebron")

Under the [United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine") passed by the UN in 1947, Hebron was envisaged to become part of an Arab state. While the Jewish leaders accepted the partition plan, the Arab leadership (the [Arab Higher Committee](/wiki/Arab_Higher_Committee "Arab Higher Committee") in Palestine and the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League")) rejected it, opposing any partition.[[276]](#cite_note-278)[[277]](#cite_note-279) The aftermath of the 1948 war saw the city occupied and later unilaterally annexed by the kingdom of [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") in a move supported by local Hebron officials. Following the Six-Day War of 1967, Israel occupied Hebron. In 1997, in accordance with the [Hebron Agreement](/wiki/Protocol_Concerning_the_Redeployment_in_Hebron "Protocol Concerning the Redeployment in Hebron"), Israel withdrew from 80 percent of Hebron, which was handed over to the Palestinian Authority. Palestinian police would assume responsibilities in Area H1 and Israel would retain control in Area H2.

An international unarmed observer force—the [Temporary International Presence in Hebron](/wiki/Temporary_International_Presence_in_Hebron "Temporary International Presence in Hebron") (TIPH) was subsequently established to help the normalization of the situation and to maintain a buffer between the Palestinian Arab population of the city and the Jewish population residing in their enclave in the old city. The TIPH operates with the permission of the Israeli government, meeting regularly with the Israeli army and the [Israeli Civil Administration](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration"), and is granted free access throughout the city. In 2018, the TIPH came under criticism in Israel due to incidents where an employee was, according to the Israeli police, filmed puncturing the tires of the car of an [Israeli settler](/wiki/Israeli_settler "Israeli settler"), and another instance where an observer was deported after slapped a settler boy.[[225]](#cite_note-20yrs-227)

The post-1967 settlement in Hebron was driven by theological doctrines from the [Mercaz HaRav Kook](/wiki/Mercaz_HaRav_Kook "Mercaz HaRav Kook"), which consider the [Land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel") and its people as holy, and believe that the messianic Age of [Redemption](/wiki/Redemption_(theology) "Redemption (theology)") has arrived. Hebron holds special significance in this narrative, with traditions linking it to [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham"), [King David](/wiki/King_David "King David"), and the entrance to the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden").[[278]](#cite_note-Røislien-280) Settling in Hebron is seen as a right and duty, a favor to the world, and an example of being "a light unto the nations" (*[Or la-Goyim](/wiki/Light_Unto_the_Nations "Light Unto the Nations")*).[[278]](#cite_note-Røislien-280) This viewpoint has resulted in numerous violent clashes with Palestinians, which some settlers see as contributing to the messianic process.

### Occupation and settlements

In 1968, Rabbi [Moshe Levinger](/wiki/Moshe_Levinger "Moshe Levinger") and a group of Israelis, disguised as tourists, rented the main hotel in Hebron and refused to leave.[[279]](#cite_note-281)[[280]](#cite_note-282) The government initially wanted to evacuate the settlers but eventually allowed them to relocate to a nearby military base, which became the settlement of [Kiryat Arba](/wiki/Kiryat_Arba "Kiryat Arba").[[281]](#cite_note-AIC04-283) After lobbying efforts, the settlement gained support from some Israeli leaders. Over time, the settlement expanded with the [outpost](/wiki/Israeli_outpost "Israeli outpost") [Givat Ha'avot](/wiki/Givat_Ha%27avot "Givat Ha'avot").[[282]](#cite_note-284) The operation was planned and financed by the [Movement for Greater Israel](/wiki/Movement_for_Greater_Israel "Movement for Greater Israel").[[283]](#cite_note-285) In 2011, the [Israeli Supreme Court](/wiki/Israeli_Supreme_Court "Israeli Supreme Court") ruled that Jews have no right to properties they possessed in places like Hebron before 1948 and are not entitled to compensation for their losses.[[153]](#cite_note-Levinsohn2011-155) Originally named Hesed l'Avraham, Beit Hadassah was constructed in 1893 with donations of [Baghdadi Jewish](/wiki/Baghdadi_Jews "Baghdadi Jews") families and was the only modern medical facility in Hebron. In 1909, it was renamed after [Hadassah Women's Zionist Organization of America](/wiki/Hadassah_Women%27s_Zionist_Organization_of_America "Hadassah Women's Zionist Organization of America"), which took responsibility for the medical staff and provided free medical care to all.[[284]](#cite_note-HbJ-286) In 1979, a group of 15 settler mothers and their 35 children squatted in the Dabouia building in Hebron, exploiting the government's indecision during [negotiations with Egypt](/wiki/Egypt%E2%80%93Israel_peace_treaty "Egypt–Israel peace treaty").[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeuman201879–80-287) Led by Miriam Levinger, they established a bridgehead for Jewish resettlement and created conflict with Arab shopkeepers.[[286]](#cite_note-:3-288) A retaliatory attack by a Palestinian group resulted in the death of six yeshiva students.[[286]](#cite_note-:3-288) Despite appeals to the Israeli Supreme Court, the settlers remained.[[287]](#cite_note-289) The following year, the government legitimized residency in Hebron and expelled the elected mayor.[[288]](#cite_note-290) This pattern of settlement followed by hostilities with Palestinians was repeated in Tel Rumeida.[[289]](#cite_note-291)[[290]](#cite_note-292)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeuman201879–80-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue%2C_Hebron.jpg/250px-Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue%2C_Hebron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue,_Hebron.jpg)

Abraham Avinu Synagogue in 1925

The [Abraham Avinu Synagogue](/wiki/Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue "Abraham Avinu Synagogue") was the physical and spiritual center of its neighborhood and regarded as one of the most beautiful synagogues in Palestine. It was the center of Jewish worship in Hebron until it was burnt down during the [1929 riots](/wiki/1929_Hebron_massacre "1929 Hebron massacre"). In 1948 under Jordanian rule, the remaining ruins were razed.[[291]](#cite_note-293) The Avraham Avinu quarter was established next to the Vegetable and Wholesale Markets on [Al-Shuhada Street](/wiki/Al-Shuhada_Street "Al-Shuhada Street") in the south of the Old City. The vegetable market was closed by the Israeli military and some of the neighboring houses were occupied by settlers and soldiers. Settlers started to take over the closed Palestinian stores, despite explicit orders of the Israeli Supreme Court that the settlers should vacate these stores and the Palestinians should be allowed to return.[[281]](#cite_note-AIC04-283) [Beit Romano](/w/index.php?title=Beit_Romano&action=edit&redlink=1 "Beit Romano (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beit_Romano "fr:Beit Romano")] was built and owned by Yisrael Avraham Romano of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and served [Sephardi Jews from Turkey](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Turkey "History of the Jews in Turkey"). In 1901, a [yeshiva](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva") was established there with a dozen teachers and up to 60 students.[[284]](#cite_note-HbJ-286) In 1982, Israeli authorities took over a Palestinian education office (Osama Ben Munqez School) and the adjacent bus station. The school was turned into a settlement, and the bus station into a military base against an order of the [Israeli Supreme Court](/wiki/Israeli_Supreme_Court "Israeli Supreme Court").[[281]](#cite_note-AIC04-283) In 1807 the immigrant Sephardic Rabbi Haim Yeshua Hamitzri (Haim the [Jewish Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptian_Jews "Egyptian Jews")) purchased 5 dunams on the outskirts of the city and in 1811 he signed a contract for a 99-year lease on a further 800 dunams of land, which included 4 plots in [Tel Rumeida](/wiki/Tel_Rumeida "Tel Rumeida"). The plots were administered by his descendant Haim Bajaio after Jews left Hebron. Settlers' claims to this land are based on these precedents, but are dismissed by the rabbi's heir.[[292]](#cite_note-294) In 1984, settlers established a caravan outpost there called Ramat Yeshai. In 1998, the government recognized it as a settlement, and in 2001 the [Defence Minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Israel) "Ministry of Defense (Israel)") approved the building of the first housing units.[[281]](#cite_note-AIC04-283)

In 2012, Israel Defense Forces called for the immediate removal of a new settlement, because it was seen as a provocation.[[293]](#cite_note-295) The IDF, in accordance with settler demands, requested the removal of a Palestinian flag on a Hebronite rooftop contiguous to settlements, though no rule forbids the practice. According to Palestinians, the IDF negotiated the removal of the flag in exchange for the release of a resident of Hebron from legal custody.[[294]](#cite_note-296) In August 2016, Israel announced its intention to allow settlement building in the military compound of *Plugat Hamitkanim* in Hebron, which had been expropriated for military purposes in the 1990s.[[295]](#cite_note-297) In late 2019, the Israeli Defense Minister [Naftali Bennett](/wiki/Naftali_Bennett "Naftali Bennett") instructed the [military administration](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration") to inform the Palestinian municipality of the government's intention to reconstruct infrastructure in the old Hebron fruit and vegetable market in order to establish a Jewish neighborhood there, which would allow for doubling the city's settler population. The area's original residents, who have protected tenancy rights there, were compelled to evacuate the zone after the [Cave of the Patriarchs massacre](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs_massacre "Cave of the Patriarchs massacre"). The original site was under Jewish ownership prior to 1948. The plan proposes that the empty shops remain Palestinian while the units built over them house Jewish Israelis.[[296]](#cite_note-298)[[297]](#cite_note-299)[[298]](#cite_note-300)

## Culture

### [Cuisine](/wiki/Cuisine "Cuisine")

See also: [Palestinian cuisine § West Bank](/wiki/Palestinian_cuisine#West_Bank "Palestinian cuisine")

The Hebron Grape Festival is a celebration held annually in Hebron since 2003 where grape farmers display their produce, with the stated goal of boosting economic activity.[[299]](#cite_note-WAFA2023-301)[[300]](#cite_note-MEE2018-302)[[301]](#cite_note-alarabiya2011-303) Besides fresh grapes, many grape preserves are also produced in Hebron, like dried grapes, grape vinegar, grape syrup, grape [fruit leather](/wiki/Fruit_leather "Fruit leather") (*[malban](/wiki/Pestil#Levant "Pestil")*), grape [murabba](/wiki/Murabba "Murabba") (*aintabikh*), and pickled unripe grapes.[[302]](#cite_note-304)[[303]](#cite_note-305)[[301]](#cite_note-alarabiya2011-303)[[304]](#cite_note-anadolu2021-306) *[Qidreh](/wiki/Qidreh "Qidreh")* is a traditional dish originating from Hebron, made from rice, meat, and [local clarified butter](/wiki/Smen "Smen").[[305]](#cite_note-307)

### Tourism

Main article: [Old City of Hebron](/wiki/Old_City_of_Hebron "Old City of Hebron")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Hebron_Old_city-souq.jpg/250px-Hebron_Old_city-souq.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hebron_Old_city-souq.jpg)

[*Souk*](/wiki/Bazaar "Bazaar") in Old City of Hebron

Hebron is home to numerous mosques, synagogues, churches, parks, palaces, castles and forts.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The [Old City of Hebron](/wiki/Old_City_of_Hebron "Old City of Hebron") was a declared a Palestinian [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") on July 7, 2017.[[307]](#cite_note-OldTownofHebron-309) The move caused controversies and faced opposition from Israeli officials who objected to it being called as Palestinian site, instead of Israeli.[[308]](#cite_note-ABC_UNESCO-310)[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) It is one of the best preserved sites of the [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk") era.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308)

* The most famous site in Hebron is the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs").[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The [Herodian era structure](/wiki/Herodian_architecture "Herodian architecture") is said to enclose the tombs of the biblical [Patriarchs](/wiki/Patriarchs "Patriarchs") and [Matriarchs](/wiki/Matriarchs_(Bible) "Matriarchs (Bible)").[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The site is known for the burial place of [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham"), [Isaac](/wiki/Isaac "Isaac") and [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob"), along with their wives [Sarah](/wiki/Sarah "Sarah"), [Rebecca](/wiki/Rebecca_(biblical_figure) "Rebecca (biblical figure)") and [Leah](/wiki/Leah "Leah") respectively.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The Isaac Hall now serves as the Ibrahimi mosque, while the Abraham and Jacob Hall serve as a synagogue.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308)
* The tombs of other biblical figures – [Abner ben Ner](/wiki/Abner "Abner"), [Otniel ben Kenaz](/wiki/Othniel "Othniel"), [Ruth](/wiki/Book_of_Ruth "Book of Ruth") and [Jesse](/wiki/Jesse_(biblical_figure) "Jesse (biblical figure)") are also located in the city.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) It is reverred to Christians, Muslims and Jews.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) These sites are located in the H2 region, which is controlled by the Israeli authorities.
* The early Ottoman-era [Abraham Avinu Synagogue](/wiki/Abraham_Avinu_Synagogue "Abraham Avinu Synagogue") in the city's historic Jewish Quarter was built in 1540 and restored in 1738.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308)
* Mosques from the era include the [Sheikh Ali al-Bakka](/wiki/Sheikh_Ali_al-Bakka_Mosque "Sheikh Ali al-Bakka Mosque") and [Al-Jawali](/wiki/Al-Jawali_Mosque "Al-Jawali Mosque") mosque.

Hebron is also home to several sites for Christian worship, with numerous churches located around the city.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The [Oak of Sibta](/wiki/Oak_of_Sibta "Oak of Sibta") (Oak of Abraham) is an ancient tree which, in non-Jewish tradition,[[309]](#cite_note-311) is said to mark the place where Abraham pitched his tent.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) The [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") owns the site and the nearby [Abraham's Oak Holy Trinity Monastery](/wiki/Abraham%27s_Oak_Holy_Trinity_Monastery "Abraham's Oak Holy Trinity Monastery"), consecrated in 1925.[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) Hebron is one of the few cities to have preserved its [Mamluk architecture](/wiki/Mamluk_architecture "Mamluk architecture").[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) Many structures were built during the period, especially Sufi [zawiyas](/wiki/Zawiya_(institution) "Zawiya (institution)").[[310]](#cite_note-312)[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308)

Other sites:

* Situated on the northeast of the city, Wadi al–Quff Natural Reserve is visited by 2,000 people, mostly on weekends.[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235) It is currently under the management of the Palestinian government.[[233]](#cite_note-:17-235)
* Aristobolia (*[Khirbet](/wiki/Khirbet "Khirbet") [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul")*), in south of Hebron, near [Zif](/wiki/Zif,_Hebron "Zif, Hebron") village, is home to Byzantine-era [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica"), built during the beginning of [Islamic era](/wiki/Islamic_History "Islamic History").[[311]](#cite_note-:16-313)
* *Khirbet al–Karmil* is home to Crusader pool, ruined Byzantine church and Crusader fortress.[[311]](#cite_note-:16-313)
* [As-Samu](/wiki/As-Samu "As-Samu") is an ancient biblical village, currently a modern town.[[311]](#cite_note-:16-313) It is home to 4th century synagogue, numerous Ottoman-era structure and an Islamic building, probably built during the time of [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") of the [Ayyubid dynasty](/wiki/Ayyubid_dynasty "Ayyubid dynasty").[[311]](#cite_note-:16-313)

### Religious traditions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Abraham%27s_Oak_Holy_Trinity_Monastery.jpg/250px-Abraham%27s_Oak_Holy_Trinity_Monastery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham%27s_Oak_Holy_Trinity_Monastery.jpg)

[Russian Orthodox Monastery in Hebron](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Forefathers_and_Monastery_of_the_Holy_Trinity "Church of the Holy Forefathers and Monastery of the Holy Trinity")

Some Jewish traditions regarding [Adam](/wiki/Adam "Adam") place him in Hebron after his expulsion from [Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden"). Another has [Cain](/wiki/Cain "Cain") kill [Abel](/wiki/Abel "Abel") there. A third has [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve") buried in the cave of Machpelah. A Jewish-Christian tradition had it that Adam was formed from the red clay of the field of [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus"), near Hebron.[[312]](#cite_note-Vilnay_1973_170–172-314)[[313]](#cite_note-315) A tradition arose in medieval Jewish texts that the Cave of the Patriarchs itself was the very entrance to the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden").[[314]](#cite_note-316) During the Middle Ages, pilgrims and the inhabitants of Hebron would eat the red earth as a charm against misfortune.[[315]](#cite_note-317)[[316]](#cite_note-318) Others report that the soil was harvested for export as a precious medicinal spice in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Arabia](/wiki/Arabia "Arabia"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") and [India](/wiki/India "India") and that the earth refilled after every digging.[[312]](#cite_note-Vilnay_1973_170–172-314) Legend also tells that [Noah](/wiki/Noah "Noah") planted his vineyard on Mount Hebron.[[317]](#cite_note-319) In [medieval Christian](/wiki/History_of_Christianity#Early_Middle_Ages_(476–842) "History of Christianity") tradition, Hebron was one of the three cities where [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(Biblical_person) "Elizabeth (Biblical person)") was said to live, the legend implying that it might have been the birthplace of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist").[[318]](#cite_note-320)[[319]](#cite_note-321)

One Islamic tradition has it that [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") alighted in Hebron during his [night journey](/wiki/Isra_and_Mi%27raj "Isra and Mi'raj") from Mecca to Jerusalem, and the mosque in the city is said to conserve one of his shoes.[[320]](#cite_note-322) Another tradition states that Muhammad arranged for Hebron and its surrounding villages to become part of [Tamim al-Dari](/wiki/Tamim_al-Dari "Tamim al-Dari")'s domain; this was implemented during [Umar](/wiki/Umar "Umar")'s reign as caliph. According to the arrangement, al-Dari and his descendants were only permitted to tax the residents for their land and the *[waqf](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf")* of the Ibrahimi Mosque was entrusted to them.[[321]](#cite_note-323) The *simat al-Khalil* or "Table of Abraham" is attested to in the writings of the 11th century [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") traveler [Nasir-i Khusraw](/wiki/Nasir-i_Khusraw "Nasir-i Khusraw").[[306]](#cite_note-:10-308) According to the account, this early Islamic food distribution center — which predates the Ottoman *[imarets](/wiki/Imaret "Imaret")* — gave all visitors to Hebron a loaf of bread, a bowl of [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil") in [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), and some [raisins](/wiki/Raisins "Raisins").[[322]](#cite_note-324)

According to Tamara Neuman, settlement by a community of Jewish religious fundamentalists has brought about three major changes by redesigning a Palestinian area in terms of biblical imagery and origins: remaking over these revamped religious sites to endow them with an innovative centrality to Jewish worship, that, she argues, effectively erases the [diasporic thrust](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") of Jewish tradition; and writing out the overlapping aspects of Judaism, Christianity and Islam in such a way that the possibility of accommodation between the three intertwined traditions is eradicated, while the presence of Palestinians themselves is erased by violent methods.[[323]](#cite_note-325)

* [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman") (Jordan)
* [Beyoğlu](/wiki/Beyo%C4%9Flu "Beyoğlu") (Turkey)
* [Bursa](/wiki/Bursa "Bursa") (Turkey)
* [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"), Morocco
* [Derby](/wiki/Derby "Derby"), England[[324]](#cite_note-326)
* [Fez](/wiki/Fez,_Morocco "Fez, Morocco") (Morocco)
* [Jajmau](/wiki/Jajmau "Jajmau") (India)
* [Keçiören](/wiki/Ke%C3%A7i%C3%B6ren "Keçiören") (Turkey)
* [Kraljevo](/wiki/Kraljevo "Kraljevo"), Serbia[[325]](#cite_note-327)
* [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina") (Saudi Arabia)
* [Saint-Pierre-des-Corps](/wiki/Saint-Pierre-des-Corps "Saint-Pierre-des-Corps") (France)
* [Şanlıurfa](/wiki/Urfa "Urfa") (Turkey)
* [Yiwu](/wiki/Yiwu "Yiwu") (China)

323. **[^](#cite_ref-325)** [Neuman 2018](#CITEREFNeuman2018), p. 5: "This narrowed or fundamentalist focus involves three further changes that are also useful for framing this study: the first is that religiously inscribed space, particularly the remaking of many Palestinian areas into a geography of biblical sites and origins, has been given a new significance in the construction of a distinct Jewish (settler) identity. Spatial reorganization has also resulted in a range of incremental practices included under the rubric of religion that link up with this process of inscription— including renaming, reenvisioning, and rebuilding. These practices in turn support and magnify resolute place-based attachments. The second shift is that these remade biblical sites, specifically in Hebron and within the Tomb of the Patriarchs itself, are being given a new centrality in Jewish observance, one that largely cancels out the exilic orientation of Jewish tradition. They give rise to a form of Jewish observance focusing on exact origins and specific graves to the exclusion of a more characteristic yearning for the messianic future. Third, the final change entails writing out the many historical convergences between Judaism, Christianity, and Islam reflected in the traditions themselves so as to eliminate possibilities for accommodating difference, while using Jewish observance and forms of direct violence in order to erase the presence of an existing Palestinian population."
